aainstitutetext: Department of Physics, McGill University, Montr├®al, QC, Canada

Extremal Effective Field Theories

Simon Caron-Huot schuot@physics.mcgill.ca a ÔÇâÔÇâ Vincent Van Duong vvd21@cam.ac.uk
Abstract

Effective field theories (EFT) parameterize the long-distance effects of short-distance dynamics whose details may or may not be known. Previous work showed that EFT coefficients must obey certain positivity constraints if causality and unitarity are satisfied at all scales. We explore those constraints from the perspective of 2ÔåÆ2ÔåÆ222\to 2 scattering amplitudes of a light real scalar field, using semi-definite programming to carve out the space of allowed EFT coefficients for a given mass threshold M­ØæÇM. We point out that all EFT parameters are bounded both below and above, effectively showing that dimensional analysis scaling is a consequence of causality. This includes the coefficients of s2+t2+u2superscript­Øæá2superscript­Øæí2superscript­Øæó2s^{2}+t^{2}+u^{2} and sÔÇïtÔÇïu­Øæá­Øæí­Øæóstu type interactions. We present simple 2ÔåÆ2ÔåÆ222\to 2 extremal amplitudes which realize, or ÔÇ£rule inÔÇØ, kinks in coefficient space and whose convex hull span a large fraction of the allowed space.

1 Introduction

The notion that degrees of freedom at different length scales decouple from each other is a cornerstone of modern physics. In this note, we consider situations where details of the short-distance physics are unknown, but one is interested in its long-distance effects as parameterized by effective field theory (EFT) coefficients. In relativistic quantum theories, it is known that ÔÇ£not anything goesÔÇØ: if the short-distance physics part is compatible with causality and unitarity, the low-energy parameters will obey certain inequalities, discussed notably in Adams:2006sv . In this paper we explore such inequalities in an effort to carve out the allowed space of local and unitary EFTs.

We will consider asymptotically flat space-time, where the S-matrix encodes long-distance or low-energy observables. We will specifically study a subset of EFT parameters, denoted gksubscript­Øæö­Øæÿg_{k}, captured by 2ÔåÆ2ÔåÆ222\to 2 scattering. As will be reviewed below, causality and unitarity imply dispersive sum rules:

gk=ÔêæJÔê½M2Ôê×­ØææsÔÇï(Ôï»)kÔÇï¤üJÔÇï(s)subscript­Øæö­Øæÿsubscript­ØÉ¢superscriptsubscriptsuperscript­ØæÇ2differential-d­ØæásubscriptÔï»­Øæÿsubscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢­Øæág_{k}=\sum_{J}\int_{M^{2}}^{\infty}ds\ (\cdots)_{k}\ \rho_{J}(s) (1)

where the spectral density ¤üJÔÇï(s)subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢­Øæá\rho_{J}(s) (proportional to the imaginary part of the amplitude) is related to the probability of a high-energy state with angular momentum J­ØÉ¢J to scatter at energy s>M­Øæá­ØæÇ\sqrt{s}>M, and the kernels (Ôï»)ksubscriptÔï»­Øæÿ(\cdots)_{k} are given explicitly below and depend on the particular EFT coefficient gksubscript­Øæö­Øæÿg_{k} of interest. The mass M­ØæÇM separates ÔÇ£lightÔÇØ and ÔÇ£heavyÔÇØ states and can be interpreted as the EFT cutoff in an appropriate scheme. We will be agnostic about the high-energy sector: our only input will be its compatibility with unitarity and crossing symmetry. Unitarity, the statement that probabilities should lie between 0 and 1, will simply mean:

0Ôëñ¤üJÔÇï(s)Ôëñ2.0subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢­Øæá20\leq\rho_{J}(s)\leq 2\,. (2)

Not all spectral densities that satisfy this inequality are reasonable candidates for the imaginary part of a scattering amplitude, however. This is because Kramers-Kronig type dispersion relations can reconstruct amplitudes from ¤üJsubscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢\rho_{J} alone, but there is no guarantee that the outcome satisfies the full crossing symmetry. Crossing-symmetric ¤üJÔÇï(s)subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢­Øæá\rho_{J}(s)ÔÇÖs are orthogonal to an infinite set of ÔÇ£null constraintsÔÇØ, which will be a key ingredient of this paper.

For our purposes, classifying causal and unitary EFTs amounts to finding the image, under the map (1), of the set of unitary and crossing-symmetric ¤üJÔÇï(s)subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢­Øæá\rho_{J}(s).

Causality constraints in quantum field theory have been discussed since the inception of the subject. Many studies were motivated by the phenomenology of the strong force Martin:1969ina . To give just a few examples, dispersion relations and sum rules were used in the analysis of low-energy pion scattering┬áRoy:1971tc ; Colangelo:2001df ; Caprini:2003ta , and inequalities satisfied by EFT parameters were obtained using properties of forward amplitudes in Pham:1985cr ; Ananthanarayan:1994hf . This work aims to explore inequalities on EFT parameters systematically.

We focus on the simplest example: a single (non-gravitating) real scalar field. Since we view the EFT cutoff M­ØæÇM as much larger than the mass of the light scattered particles, we take the latter to be massless. On grounds of dimensional analysis, one expects the coefficient of a (k+d)­Øæÿ­Øææ(k+d)-dimensional operator in the low-energy effective Lagrangian to scale like Ôê╝1/Mksimilar-toabsent1superscript­ØæÇ­Øæÿ\sim 1/M^{k}, possibly further suppressed by a small coupling, but never larger. This scaling is clearly realized when one integrates out a massive field. The main question to be addressed is: Can dimensional analysis scaling be justified by rigorous numerical bounds? Can ÔÇ£accidentally largeÔÇØ EFT coefficients be ruled out?

We will find that the answer is positive, and we present a general framework to numerically obtain the optimal bounds. Furthermore, we will show that much of the shape of the allowed space, including two kinks, can be understood from simple analytic scattering amplitudes.

The relation between dimensional analysis scaling and causality resonates with many previous studies, for example Camanho:2014apa ; Afkhami-Jeddi:2016ntf ; Cheung:2016yqr ; talks ; deRham:2017avq . Our new observation will be the seemingly universal existence of two-sided bounds.

This paper is organized as follows. In section 2 we review the general principles satisfied by scattering amplitudes, introducing a family of ÔÇ£Bksubscript­ØÉÁ­ØæÿB_{k}ÔÇØ sum rules expressing EFT coefficients as averages over high-energy probabilities. In section 3, we provide a general numerical optimization strategy to rule-out candidate EFTs by making use of the averaging technology. In section 4, numerical results are presented along with remarks. Section 5 bridges the numerics with the analytic results. We conclude in section 6 with a discussion about the potential use cases of the numerical framework presented and the further implications of the numerical results.

Note added: When this manuscript was being completed, the works Bellazzini:2020cot and then Tolley:2020gtv appeared with partial overlap in the results. The second paper in particular gave a two-sided bound on the sÔÇïtÔÇïu­Øæá­Øæí­Øæóstu interaction which agrees with our eq.┬á(31). Further comparisons will be interesting.

2 Preliminaries: Scattering amplitudes and dispersion relations

2.1 Low energy: effective field theory

We consider 2ÔåÆ2ÔåÆ222\to 2 scattering of massless identical real scalars in a Poincar├® invariant theory (fig.┬á1). Treating all momenta as incoming, the amplitude is a function of Mandelstam invariants:

s=ÔêÆ(p1+p2)2,t=ÔêÆ(p2+p3)2,u=ÔêÆ(p1+p3)2formulae-sequence­Øæásuperscriptsubscript­ØæØ1subscript­ØæØ22formulae-sequence­Øæísuperscriptsubscript­ØæØ2subscript­ØæØ32­Øæósuperscriptsubscript­ØæØ1subscript­ØæØ32\displaystyle s=-(p_{1}+p_{2})^{2},\quad t=-(p_{2}+p_{3})^{2},\quad u=-(p_{1}+p_{3})^{2} (3)

which satisfy s+t+u=0­Øæá­Øæí­Øæó0s+t+u=0. By crossing symmetry, it is invariant under all permutations (this holds with appropriate iÔÇï0­Øæû0i0ÔÇÖs in the discontinuity, as further discussed below):

Ôä│ÔÇï(s,t)=Ôä│ÔÇï(t,s)=Ôä│ÔÇï(s,u)=ÔÇªÔä│­Øæá­ØæíÔä│­Øæí­ØæáÔä│­Øæá­ØæóÔÇª\mathcal{M}(s,t)=\mathcal{M}(t,s)=\mathcal{M}(s,u)=\dots (4)

Our first step is to parameterize the amplitude at low energies in terms of a specific effective field theory. Generally, the form of the amplitude depends on the couplings of the theory. It becomes particularly simple if the theory is weakly coupled and we restrict ourselves to the tree approximation. We thus use the tree approximation here and until subsection 2.4 In this case, the amplitude has no low-energy branch-cuts, so the EFT expansion is simply a series in small s,t,u­Øæá­Øæí­Øæós,t,u:

Ôä│lowÔÇï(s,t)=ÔêÆg2ÔÇï[1s+1t+1u]ÔêÆ╬╗+g2ÔÇï(s2+t2+u2)+g3ÔÇï(sÔÇïtÔÇïu)+g4ÔÇï(s2+t2+u2)2+g5ÔÇï(s2+t2+u2)ÔÇï(sÔÇïtÔÇïu)+g6ÔÇï(s2+t2+u2)3+g6ÔÇ▓ÔÇï(sÔÇïtÔÇïu)2+g7ÔÇï(s2+t2+u2)2ÔÇï(sÔÇïtÔÇïu)+Ôï»subscriptÔä│low­Øæá­Øæísuperscript­Øæö2delimited-[]1­Øæá1­Øæí1­Øæó­Ø£åsubscript­Øæö2superscript­Øæá2superscript­Øæí2superscript­Øæó2subscript­Øæö3­Øæá­Øæí­Øæósubscript­Øæö4superscriptsuperscript­Øæá2superscript­Øæí2superscript­Øæó22subscript­Øæö5superscript­Øæá2superscript­Øæí2superscript­Øæó2­Øæá­Øæí­Øæósubscript­Øæö6superscriptsuperscript­Øæá2superscript­Øæí2superscript­Øæó23superscriptsubscript­Øæö6ÔÇ▓superscript­Øæá­Øæí­Øæó2subscript­Øæö7superscriptsuperscript­Øæá2superscript­Øæí2superscript­Øæó22­Øæá­Øæí­ØæóÔï»\displaystyle\begin{split}\mathcal{M}_{\text{low}}(s,t)=&-g^{2}\left[\frac{1}{s}+\frac{1}{t}+\frac{1}{u}\right]-\lambda\\ &+g_{2}(s^{2}+t^{2}+u^{2})+g_{3}(stu)+g_{4}(s^{2}+t^{2}+u^{2})^{2}+g_{5}(s^{2}+t^{2}+u^{2})(stu)\\ &+g_{6}(s^{2}+t^{2}+u^{2})^{3}+g_{6}^{\prime}(stu)^{2}+g_{7}(s^{2}+t^{2}+u^{2})^{2}(stu)+\cdots\end{split} (5)

The first line accounts for ¤ò3superscriptitalic-¤ò3\phi^{3} and ¤ò4superscriptitalic-¤ò4\phi^{4} relevant interactions, while the remaining terms simply list the most general symmetric polynomials in s,t,u­Øæá­Øæí­Øæós,t,u, to account for higher-dimension operators in the EFT. The subscript denotes the degree in Mandelstam invariants. Symmetric polynomials are easy to enumerate since their ring is freely generated by two elements: s2+t2+u2superscript­Øæá2superscript­Øæí2superscript­Øæó2s^{2}+t^{2}+u^{2} and sÔÇïtÔÇïu­Øæá­Øæí­Øæóstu (given that s+t+u=0­Øæá­Øæí­Øæó0s+t+u=0).

A short exercise shows that the preceding amplitude is obtained from the following effective Lagrangian in the tree approximation:

ÔäÆlow=subscriptÔäÆlowabsent\displaystyle\mathcal{L}_{\rm low}= ÔêÆ12ÔÇï(Ôêé╬╝¤ò)2ÔêÆg3!ÔÇï¤ò3ÔêÆ╬╗4!ÔÇï¤ò412superscriptsubscript­Ø£çitalic-¤ò2­Øæö3superscriptitalic-¤ò3­Ø£å4superscriptitalic-¤ò4\displaystyle-\frac{1}{2}(\partial_{\mu}\phi)^{2}-\frac{g}{3!}\phi^{3}-\frac{\lambda}{4!}\phi^{4} (6)
+g22ÔÇï[(Ôêé╬╝¤ò)2]2+g33ÔÇï(Ôêé╬╝Ôêé╬¢¤ò)2ÔÇï(Ôêé¤â¤ò)2+4ÔÇïg4ÔÇï[(Ôêé╬╝Ôêé╬¢¤ò)2]2+Ôï»subscript­Øæö22superscriptdelimited-[]superscriptsubscript­Ø£çitalic-¤ò22subscript­Øæö33superscriptsubscript­Ø£çsubscript­Ø£êitalic-¤ò2superscriptsubscript­Ø£Äitalic-¤ò24subscript­Øæö4superscriptdelimited-[]superscriptsubscript­Ø£çsubscript­Ø£êitalic-¤ò22Ôï»\displaystyle+\frac{g_{2}}{2}\big{[}(\partial_{\mu}\phi)^{2}\big{]}^{2}+\frac{g_{3}}{3}(\partial_{\mu}\partial_{\nu}\phi)^{2}(\partial_{\sigma}\phi)^{2}+4g_{4}\big{[}(\partial_{\mu}\partial_{\nu}\phi)^{2}\big{]}^{2}+\cdots

As is well known, Lagrangian densities are not unique: they are defined modulo integration-by-parts and field redefinitions. One can cast any effective Lagrangian for a real scalar field into the form (6) by using field redefinitions to eliminate, order by order in the derivative expansion, corrections to the kinetic and cubic terms as well as appearances of Ôêé2¤òsuperscript2italic-¤ò\partial^{2}\phi. See for example Brivio:2017vri for a discussion in the Standard Model context. The amplitude (5) is a physical observable unaffected by such ambiguities, which is why we choose to parameterize the coefficients in terms of it.

Our goal is to constrain the EFT parameters gksubscript­Øæö­Øæÿg_{k} assuming existence of an high-energy completion which is causal and unitary, but not necessarily weakly coupled. Low-energy interactions involving five or more powers of ¤òitalic-¤ò\phi will not be constrained by our methods, since they are not detected by (tree-level) 2ÔåÆ2ÔåÆ222\to 2 scattering. When low-energy loop corrections are included, the detailed form of eq.┬á(5) will be modified, but we do not expect the number of independent EFT parameters that we can constrain to increase. A precise definition of the gksubscript­Øæö­Øæÿg_{k}ÔÇÖs that remains valid in the presence of low-energy loop corrections is given in eq.┬á(20) below.

2.2 High energy: partial wave decomposition

At high energies, we will be agnostic about the form of the amplitude except for the assumption that it is causal and unitary. We follow the general framework of S-matrix theory, as reviewed for example in Martin:1969ina . Let us begin with unitarity of the S-matrix, which is formally that: SÔÇáÔÇïS=1superscript­ØæåÔÇá­Øæå1S^{\dagger}S=1, where S=1+iÔÇïÔä│­Øæå1­ØæûÔä│S=1+i\mathcal{M}. More precisely, one picks a physical region, say where s>0­Øæá0s>0 is interpreted as center-of-mass energy squared, and ÔêÆs<t<0­Øæá­Øæí0-s<t<0 gives the momentum transfer (squared); the scattering angle is

cosÔüí╬©=1+2ÔÇïts.­Ø£â12­Øæí­Øæá\cos\theta=1+\frac{2t}{s}\,. (7)

The scattering operator is a convolution with respect to angles, which is diagonalized by going to a basis of angular momentum partial waves. The unitarity condition is thus simplest to state in this basis (our conventions follow Correia:2020xtr ):

Ôä│ÔÇï(s,t)=ÔêæJÔÇï┬áeven┬ánJ(d)ÔÇïfJÔÇï(s)ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïts),nJ(d)=(4ÔÇï¤Ç)d2ÔÇï(d+2ÔÇïJÔêÆ3)ÔÇï╬ôÔÇï(d+JÔêÆ3)¤ÇÔÇï╬ôÔÇï(dÔêÆ22)ÔÇï╬ôÔÇï(J+1),formulae-sequenceÔä│­Øæá­Øæísubscript­ØÉ¢┬áeven┬ásuperscriptsubscript­Øæø­ØÉ¢­Øææsubscript­Øæô­ØÉ¢­Øæásubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢12­Øæí­Øæásuperscriptsubscript­Øæø­ØÉ¢­Øææsuperscript4­Ø£ï­Øææ2­Øææ2­ØÉ¢3╬ô­Øææ­ØÉ¢3­Ø£ï╬ô­Øææ22╬ô­ØÉ¢1\mathcal{M}(s,t)=\sum_{J\text{ even }}n_{J}^{(d)}f_{J}(s)\mathcal{P}_{J}\left(1+\tfrac{2t}{s}\right),\quad n_{J}^{(d)}=\frac{(4\pi)^{\frac{d}{2}}(d+2J-3)\Gamma(d+J-3)}{\pi\Gamma\left(\frac{d-2}{2}\right)\Gamma(J+1)}\,, (8)

where d­Øææd is the space-time dimensions and ­ØÆ½JÔÇï(x)subscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢­ØæÑ\mathcal{P}_{J}(x) are the d­Øææd-dimensional version of Legendre polynomials (which appear in the d=4­Øææ4d=4 case):

­ØÆ½JÔÇï(x)ÔëíF12ÔÇï(ÔêÆJ,J+dÔêÆ3,dÔêÆ22,1ÔêÆx2).subscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢­ØæÑsubscriptsubscript­ØÉ╣12­ØÉ¢­ØÉ¢­Øææ3­Øææ221­ØæÑ2\mathcal{P}_{J}(x)\equiv{}_{2}F_{1}\left(-J,J+d-3,\frac{d-2}{2},\frac{1-x}{2}\right)\,. (9)

Details of the specific scattering process are encoded in the coefficients fJÔÇï(s)subscript­Øæô­ØÉ¢­Øæáf_{J}(s). In this normalization convention, unitarity of the elastic amplitude of identical real particles is Correia:2020xtr :

|SJÔÇï(s)|Ôëñ1,SJÔÇï(s)Ôëí1+iÔÇïsdÔêÆ42ÔÇïfJÔÇï(s).formulae-sequencesubscript­Øæå­ØÉ¢­Øæá1subscript­Øæå­ØÉ¢­Øæá1­Øæûsuperscript­Øæá­Øææ42subscript­Øæô­ØÉ¢­Øæá\big{|}S_{J}(s)\big{|}\leq 1,\qquad S_{J}(s)\equiv 1+is^{\frac{d-4}{2}}f_{J}(s)\,. (10)

The elastic amplitude SJsubscript­Øæå­ØÉ¢S_{J} can have absolute value less than unity due to inelastic processes. We will only need the imaginary part of the high-energy amplitude. Defining the spectral density ¤üJÔÇï(s)=sdÔêÆ42ÔÇïImÔÇïfJÔÇï(s)subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢­Øæásuperscript­Øæá­Øææ42Imsubscript­Øæô­ØÉ¢­Øæá\rho_{J}(s)=s^{\frac{d-4}{2}}{\rm Im\ }f_{J}(s), it can thus be written as

ImÔÇïÔä│ÔÇï(s,t)=s4ÔêÆd2ÔÇïÔêæJÔÇï┬áeven┬ánJ(d)ÔÇï¤üJÔÇï(s)ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïts)ImÔä│­Øæá­Øæísuperscript­Øæá4­Øææ2subscript­ØÉ¢┬áeven┬ásuperscriptsubscript­Øæø­ØÉ¢­Øææsubscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢­Øæásubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢12­Øæí­Øæá{\rm Im}\ \mathcal{M}(s,t)=s^{\frac{4-d}{2}}\sum_{J\text{ even }}n_{J}^{(d)}\ \rho_{J}(s)\mathcal{P}_{J}\left(1+\tfrac{2t}{s}\right) (11)

where the unitarity constraint is

0Ôëñ¤üJÔÇï(s)Ôëñ2ÔêÇs>0,ÔêÇJÔÇïeven.formulae-sequence0subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢­Øæá2for-all­Øæá0for-all­ØÉ¢even0\leq\rho_{J}(s)\leq 2\qquad\forall s>0,\forall J\ {\rm even}\,. (12)

The normalization is such that ¤üJ=1subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢1\rho_{J}=1 for complete absorption (SJ=0subscript­Øæå­ØÉ¢0S_{J}=0), and ¤üJ=2subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢2\rho_{J}=2 for an elastic phase shift ¤Ç­Ø£ï\pi. For the most part (except for subsection 3.5) we will only use the first inequality: 0Ôëñ¤üJÔÇï(s)0subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢­Øæá0\leq\rho_{J}(s).

2.3 Dispersion relations

The other key ingredient from S-matrix theory is the connection between low and high energies, which stems from analyticity. More precisely, we will use the following two properties of the amplitude:

  1. 1.

    For fixed t<0­Øæí0t<0 and |s|­Øæá|s| sufficiently large, Ôä│ÔÇï(s,t)Ôä│­Øæá­Øæí\mathcal{M}(s,t) is analytic in s­Øæás away from the real axis.

  2. 2.

    For fixed t<0­Øæí0t<0, lim|s|ÔåÆÔê×|Ôä│ÔÇï(s,t)s2|=0subscriptÔåÆ­ØæáÔä│­Øæá­Øæísuperscript­Øæá20\lim_{|s|\to\infty}\left|\frac{\mathcal{M}(s,t)}{s^{2}}\right|=0 along any line of constant phase.

Physically, these conditions combine causality and unitarity. For an elementary explanation of their respective significance, we refer to the signal propagation model in appendix D of Camanho:2014apa , where it is explained that propagation of a signal through a black box is causal if and only if the corresponding transfer function SÔÇï(¤ë)­Øæå­Ø£öS(\omega) is analytic in the upper-half frequency plane (with sub-exponential growth), and that |SÔÇï(¤ë)|2Ôëñ1superscript­Øæå­Ø£ö21|S(\omega)|^{2}\leq 1 throughout the upper-half-plane if the box furthermore preserves the squared-norm of signals. These are general facts about Fourier transforms. The original physical derivation of crossing symmetry GellMann:1954db applies these facts to the expectation value of a retarded commutator in one-particle state. Schematically, one considers

Ôƒ¿p4|[¤òÔÇï(x3),¤òÔÇï(x2)]ÔÇï╬©ÔÇï(x30ÔêÆx20)|p1Ôƒ®,quantum-operator-productsubscript­ØæØ4italic-¤òsubscript­ØæÑ3italic-¤òsubscript­ØæÑ2­Ø£âsuperscriptsubscript­ØæÑ30superscriptsubscript­ØæÑ20subscript­ØæØ1\langle p_{4}|\ [\phi(x_{3}),\phi(x_{2})]\theta(x_{3}^{0}-x_{2}^{0})\ |p_{1}\rangle, (13)

which vanishes outside the forward light-cone rendering its Fourier transform analytic in the upper-half s­Øæás-plane (intuitively, one uses that s­Øæás is linear in right-moving light-cone momentum), at least for large enough |s|­Øæá|s|. Its boundary values on the real axis unite the s­Øæás-channel amplitude and the complex conjugate of the u­Øæóu-channel amplitude, see fig.┬á2.

This is the traditional understanding of crossing symmetry within the axiomatic theory Bros:1965kbd . The boundedness property, in particular including the extra factor of 1/s21superscript­Øæá21/s^{2} compared with the signal model, will be critical for us. We believe it can be justified physically by directly analyzing the transverse Fourier transform SCH:bootstrap2020 . As far as we understand, properties 1-2 are theorems in axiomatic quantum field theory, for example in the context of pion scattering Jin:1964zza ; Martin:1965jj ; we take them as axioms embodying causality and unitarity.

The two properties assumed above amount to the existence of twice-subtracted dispersion relations. Let us derive such dispersion relations explicitly. The starting point is that an integral over a large circle vanishes:

Ôê«Ôê×dÔÇïsÔÇ▓2ÔÇï¤ÇÔÇïiÔÇï(sÔÇ▓ÔêÆs)ÔÇïÔä│ÔÇï(sÔÇ▓,t)(sÔÇ▓ÔêÆs1)ÔÇï(sÔÇ▓ÔêÆs2)=0subscriptcontour-integral­Øææsuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓2­Ø£ï­Øæûsuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­ØæáÔä│superscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­Øæísuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓subscript­Øæá1superscript­ØæáÔÇ▓subscript­Øæá20\oint_{\infty}\frac{ds^{\prime}}{2\pi i(s^{\prime}-s)}\frac{\mathcal{M}(s^{\prime},t)}{(s^{\prime}-s_{1})(s^{\prime}-s_{2})}=0 (14)

where s1subscript­Øæá1s_{1} and s2subscript­Øæá2s_{2} are arbitrary subtraction points. For large enough sÔÇ▓superscript­ØæáÔÇ▓s^{\prime}, the integrand behaves like Ôê╝Ôä│ÔÇï(sÔÇ▓,t)/sÔÇ▓Ôüú3similar-toabsentÔä│superscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­Øæísuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓3\sim\mathcal{M}(s^{\prime},t)/s^{\prime 3}, and so the integral vanishes thanks to property 2. Typically, one would formally treat all of s­Øæás, s1subscript­Øæá1s_{1} and s2subscript­Øæá2s_{2} as non-real and deform the contour toward the real axis. Summing the three explicit poles and cuts then relates Ôä│ÔÇï(s,t)Ôä│­Øæá­Øæí\mathcal{M}(s,t) to its value at two subtraction points plus an integral over the discontinuity of Ôä│Ôä│\mathcal{M} across the real axis Eden:1966dnq .

For our purposes it is convenient to instead treat the subtraction poles as part of the real-axis cuts. We choose s1=0subscript­Øæá10s_{1}=0 and s2=ÔêÆtsubscript­Øæá2­Øæís_{2}=-t to maintain the symmetry between the s­Øæás and u­Øæóu channels without introducing any new energy scale into the problem. The identity (14) then relates the residue at sÔÇ▓=ssuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­Øæás^{\prime}=s with a discontinuity:

Ôä│ÔÇï(s,t)sÔÇï(s+t)=Ôê½ÔêÆÔê×Ôê×dÔÇïsÔÇ▓¤ÇÔÇï(sÔÇ▓ÔêÆs)ÔÇïImÔÇï[Ôä│ÔÇï(sÔÇ▓,t)sÔÇ▓ÔÇï(sÔÇ▓+t)](t<0,sÔêëÔäØ),Ôä│­Øæá­Øæí­Øæá­Øæá­Øæísuperscriptsubscript­Øææsuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­Ø£ïsuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­ØæáImdelimited-[]Ôä│superscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­Øæísuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓superscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­Øæíformulae-sequence­Øæí0­ØæáÔäØ\frac{\mathcal{M}(s,t)}{s(s+t)}=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\frac{ds^{\prime}}{\pi(s^{\prime}-s)}\ {\rm Im}\left[\frac{\mathcal{M}(s^{\prime},t)}{s^{\prime}(s^{\prime}+t)}\right]\qquad(t<0,s\notin\mathbb{R}), (15)

where we have written the discontinuity as an imaginary since the amplitude on the ÔÇ£wrong side of the cutÔÇØ is its complex conjugate; technically ImÔÇïfÔÇï(s)Ôëí12ÔÇïiÔÇï[fÔÇï(s+iÔÇï0)ÔêÆfÔÇï(sÔêÆiÔÇï0)]Im­Øæô­Øæá12­Øæûdelimited-[]­Øæô­Øæá­Øæû0­Øæô­Øæá­Øæû0{\rm Im}\ f(s)\equiv\tfrac{1}{2i}\big{[}f(s+i0)-f(s-i0)\big{]}. We call eq.┬á(15) a twice-subtracted dispersion relation because of the two powers of sÔÇ▓superscript­ØæáÔÇ▓s^{\prime} added to the denominator.

Let us see how this works in the simplest situation considered in eq.┬á(5), where low-energy loops are neglected. Then branch cuts can only start at the UV cutoff M2superscript­ØæÇ2M^{2}. The right-hand-side of eq.┬á(15) then contains low-energy poles at s=0­Øæá0s=0 and s=ÔêÆt­Øæá­Øæís=-t (due both to the denominator in eq.┬á(15) and poles in the amplitude), and high-energy cuts at s>M2­Øæásuperscript­ØæÇ2s>M^{2} and s<ÔêÆM2ÔêÆt­Øæásuperscript­ØæÇ2­Øæís<-M^{2}-t. Separating low and high energies gives a relation:

Ôä│lowÔÇï(s,t)sÔÇï(s+t)+RessÔÇ▓=0,ÔêÆt[1sÔÇ▓ÔêÆsÔÇïÔä│lowÔÇï(sÔÇ▓,t)sÔÇ▓ÔÇï(sÔÇ▓+t)]subscriptÔä│low­Øæá­Øæí­Øæá­Øæá­ØæísubscriptRessuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓0­Øæí1superscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­ØæásubscriptÔä│lowsuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­Øæísuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓superscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­Øæí\displaystyle\frac{\mathcal{M}_{\rm low}(s,t)}{s(s+t)}+\operatorname*{Res}\limits_{s^{\prime}=0,-t}\left[\frac{1}{s^{\prime}-s}\frac{\mathcal{M}_{\rm low}(s^{\prime},t)}{s^{\prime}(s^{\prime}+t)}\right] (16)
=Ôê½M2Ôê×dÔÇïsÔÇ▓¤ÇÔÇï(1sÔÇ▓ÔêÆs+1sÔÇ▓+s+t)ÔÇïImÔÇï[Ôä│highÔÇï(sÔÇ▓,t)sÔÇ▓ÔÇï(sÔÇ▓+t)].absentsuperscriptsubscriptsuperscript­ØæÇ2­Øææsuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­Ø£ï1superscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­Øæá1superscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­Øæá­ØæíImdelimited-[]subscriptÔä│highsuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­Øæísuperscript­ØæáÔÇ▓superscript­ØæáÔÇ▓­Øæí\displaystyle\qquad=\int_{M^{2}}^{\infty}\frac{ds^{\prime}}{\pi}\left(\frac{1}{s^{\prime}-s}+\frac{1}{s^{\prime}+s+t}\right)\ {\rm Im}\left[\frac{\mathcal{M}_{\rm high}(s^{\prime},t)}{s^{\prime}(s^{\prime}+t)}\right]\,.

We used sÔåöuÔåö­Øæá­Øæós\leftrightarrow u symmetry to combine the left and right cuts. This relation is supposed to converge for any s,t­Øæá­Øæís,t with u<0­Øæó0u<0. Interestingly, plugging in the EFT expansion eq.┬á(5) for Ôä│lowsubscriptÔä│low\mathcal{M}_{\rm low}, one finds that both the spin-0 exchange diagram g2superscript­Øæö2g^{2} and spin-0 contact interaction ╬╗­Ø£å\lambda cancel out, and what remains is pole-free (this could have been anticipated from the fact that the three residues on the left combine into a single contour over a large circle). On the right-hand-side we insert the partial wave expansion (11). It is useful to define heavy averages:

Ôƒ¿FÔÇï(m2,J)Ôƒ®ÔëíÔêæJÔÇï┬áeven┬ánJ(d)ÔÇïÔê½M2Ôê×dÔÇïm2¤ÇÔÇïm4ÔêÆdm2ÔÇï¤üJÔÇï(m2)ÔÇï[FÔÇï(m2,J)].delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®­ØÉ╣superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢subscript­ØÉ¢┬áeven┬ásuperscriptsubscript­Øæø­ØÉ¢­Øææsuperscriptsubscriptsuperscript­ØæÇ2­Øææsuperscript­ØæÜ2­Ø£ïsuperscript­ØæÜ4­Øææsuperscript­ØæÜ2subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢superscript­ØæÜ2delimited-[]­ØÉ╣superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢\left\langle F(m^{2},J)\right\rangle\equiv\sum_{J\text{ even }}n_{J}^{(d)}\int_{M^{2}}^{\infty}\frac{dm^{2}}{\pi}\frac{m^{4-d}}{m^{2}}\rho_{J}(m^{2})\left[F(m^{2},J)\right]. (17)

Eq.┬á(16) becomes, for t<0­Øæí0t<0:

2g2ÔêÆtg3+4(2t2+s(s+t))g4+ÔÇª=Ôƒ¿2ÔÇïm2+t(m2ÔêÆs)ÔÇï(m2+s+t)­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïtm2)m2ÔÇï(m2+t)Ôƒ®.2g_{2}-tg_{3}+4\big{(}2t^{2}+s(s+t)\big{)}g_{4}+\ldots\quad=\left\langle\frac{2m^{2}+t}{(m^{2}-s)(m^{2}+s+t)}\frac{\mathcal{P}_{J}(1+\frac{2t}{m^{2}})}{m^{2}(m^{2}+t)}\right\rangle\,. (18)

The averaging symbol denotes a (non-normalized) positive sum over heavy states with mass m>M­ØæÜ­ØæÇm>M. All the results in this paper follow from Taylor-expanding both sides in s­Øæás and t­Øæít and using positivity of the measure Ôƒ¿Ôï»Ôƒ®delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®Ôï»\left\langle\cdots\right\rangle. It will be useful (though non-essential) to re-organize a bit.

2.4 The BkÔÇï(t)subscript­ØÉÁ­Øæÿ­ØæíB_{k}(t) family of sum rules

It is easy to see that the Taylor expansion of both sides of eq.┬á(18) maintains the symmetry under sÔåÆÔêÆsÔêÆtÔåÆ­Øæá­Øæá­Øæís\to-s-t, and therefore only even powers of s­Øæás carry information. More precisely, for each even integer k­Øæÿk the coefficient of [sÔÇï(s+t)]k/2ÔêÆ1superscriptdelimited-[]­Øæá­Øæá­Øæí­Øæÿ21[s(s+t)]^{k/2-1} gives a one-parameter family of sum rules parameterized by t­Øæít, which we call BkÔÇï(t)subscript­ØÉÁ­Øæÿ­ØæíB_{k}(t). It can be computed by taking the sÔåÆ0ÔåÆ­Øæá0s\to 0 limit in eq.┬á(14):

BkÔÇï(t)ÔëíÔê«Ôê×dÔÇïs2ÔÇï¤ÇÔÇïiÔÇï1sÔÇïÔä│ÔÇï(s,t)[sÔÇï(s+t)]k/2=0(t<0,k=2,4,ÔÇª).formulae-sequencesubscript­ØÉÁ­Øæÿ­Øæísubscriptcontour-integral­Øææ­Øæá2­Ø£ï­Øæû1­ØæáÔä│­Øæá­Øæísuperscriptdelimited-[]­Øæá­Øæá­Øæí­Øæÿ20formulae-sequence­Øæí0­Øæÿ24ÔÇªB_{k}(t)\equiv\oint_{\infty}\frac{ds}{2\pi i}\frac{1}{s}\ \frac{\mathcal{M}(s,t)}{\big{[}s(s+t)\big{]}^{k/2}}=0\qquad\qquad(t<0,\ k=2,4,\ldots)\,. (19)

This is similar to moment sum rules Ôê«Ôê×dÔÇïssk+1ÔÇïÔä│ÔÇï(s,t)subscriptcontour-integral­Øææ­Øæásuperscript­Øæá­Øæÿ1Ôä│­Øæá­Øæí\oint_{\infty}\frac{ds}{s^{k+1}}\mathcal{M}(s,t) which have been used since times immemorial. Here we have simply re-organized using the sÔåöuÔåö­Øæá­Øæós\leftrightarrow u symmetry of our problem to eliminate odd moments.111 The identity: Ôê«Ôê×dÔÇïs2ÔÇï¤ÇÔÇïiÔÇï1sÔÇï[sÔÇï(s+t)](kÔêÆkÔÇ▓)/2=╬┤k,kÔÇ▓subscriptcontour-integral­Øææ­Øæá2­Ø£ï­Øæû1­Øæásuperscriptdelimited-[]­Øæá­Øæá­Øæí­Øæÿsuperscript­ØæÿÔÇ▓2subscript­Øø┐­Øæÿsuperscript­ØæÿÔÇ▓\oint_{\infty}\frac{ds}{2\pi i}\frac{1}{s}\big{[}s(s+t)\big{]}^{(k-k^{\prime})/2}=\delta_{k,k^{\prime}} shows that eq.┬á(19) indeed extracts the coefficient of [sÔÇï(s+t)]k/2superscriptdelimited-[]­Øæá­Øæá­Øæí­Øæÿ2[s(s+t)]^{k/2} in Ôä│lowsubscriptÔä│low\mathcal{M}_{\rm low}. The subscript indicates that Bksubscript­ØÉÁ­ØæÿB_{k} enjoys the high-energy convergence of a k­Øæÿk-subtracted dispersion relation.

A closely related basis of sum rules was introduced recently for conformal field theory correlators Caron-Huot:2020adz (see also Penedones:2019tng ). For holographic theories, the Mellin-space form of the sum rule, called B^k,tsubscript^­ØÉÁ­Øæÿ­Øæí\widehat{B}_{k,t} (see eq.┬á(4.54) and section 4.8 there), precisely reduces in the flat space limit to our current BkÔÇï(t)subscript­ØÉÁ­Øæÿ­ØæíB_{k}(t). In this context, convergence for kÔëÑ2­Øæÿ2k\geq 2 is a consequence of the known boundedness of conformal correlators in the Regge limit.

For massless scattering, the low-energy s­Øæás- and u­Øæóu-channel cuts of Ôä│ÔÇï(s,t)Ôä│­Øæá­Øæí\mathcal{M}(s,t) generally overlap as shown in fig.┬á2. It is important that eq.┬á(19) can be computed without going between the cuts. We simply deform the contour to pick heavy branch cuts at s>M2­Øæásuperscript­ØæÇ2s>M^{2} and u>M2­Øæósuperscript­ØæÇ2u>M^{2}, and keep the rest as large arcs with |s|Ôê╝M2similar-to­Øæásuperscript­ØæÇ2|s|\sim M^{2}, the EFT cutoff, see fig.┬á4. This gives a relation between physics at the scale M­ØæÇM and that at higher-energies:

Bk:Ôê«|s|ÔëêM2dÔÇïs2ÔÇï¤ÇÔÇïiÔÇï1sÔÇïÔä│ÔÇï(s,t)[sÔÇï(s+t)]k/2=Ôƒ¿2ÔÇïm2+tm2+tÔÇï­ØÆ½J(d)ÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïtm2)[m2ÔÇï(m2+t)]k/2Ôƒ®(t<0,k=2,4,ÔÇª).:subscript­ØÉÁ­Øæÿsubscriptcontour-integral­Øæásuperscript­ØæÇ2­Øææ­Øæá2­Ø£ï­Øæû1­ØæáÔä│­Øæá­Øæísuperscriptdelimited-[]­Øæá­Øæá­Øæí­Øæÿ2delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®2superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísuperscriptsubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢­Øææ12­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2superscriptdelimited-[]superscript­ØæÜ2superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæí­Øæÿ2formulae-sequence­Øæí0­Øæÿ24ÔÇªB_{k}:\ \oint_{|s|\approx M^{2}}\frac{ds}{2\pi i}\frac{1}{s}\frac{\mathcal{M}(s,t)}{\big{[}s(s+t)\big{]}^{k/2}}=\left\langle\frac{2m^{2}+t}{m^{2}+t}\frac{\mathcal{P}_{J}^{(d)}\left(1+\tfrac{2t}{m^{2}}\right)}{\big{[}m^{2}(m^{2}+t)\big{]}^{k/2}}\right\rangle\qquad(t<0,\ k=2,4,\ldots)\,. (20)

This equation is valid even when EFT loops are included. The idea is to choose the EFT cutoff M­ØæÇM such that loop corrections in the low-energy EFT are under control over the arcs with |s|ÔëêM2­Øæásuperscript­ØæÇ2|s|\approx M^{2}. Eq.┬á(20) thus equates an EFT-computable left-hand-side, with a high-energy average that enjoys positivity properties.

The specific relation between the left-hand-side and EFT coefficients will depend on EFT interactions. For concreteness let us thus focus again on the case where EFT loops are neglected. The left-hand-side is then just the sum of residues at s=0­Øæá0s=0 and s=ÔêÆt­Øæá­Øæís=-t; By symmetry, we can replace 1s1­Øæá\frac{1}{s} by (1sÔêÆ1s+t)1­Øæá1­Øæá­Øæí\left(\frac{1}{s}-\frac{1}{s+t}\right) and include a single pole, and the Bksubscript­ØÉÁ­ØæÿB_{k} sum rules becomes:

Bk:Ress=0[2ÔÇïs+tsÔÇï(s+t)Ôä│lowÔÇï(s,t)[sÔÇï(s+t)]k/2]=Ôƒ¿2ÔÇïm2+tm2+t­ØÆ½J(d)ÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïtm2)[m2ÔÇï(m2+t)]k/2Ôƒ®(t<0,k=2,4,ÔÇª).\boxed{B_{k}:\ \operatorname*{Res}\limits_{s=0}\left[\frac{2s+t}{s(s+t)}\frac{\mathcal{M}_{\text{low}}(s,t)}{\big{[}s(s+t)\big{]}^{k/2}}\right]=\left\langle\frac{2m^{2}+t}{m^{2}+t}\frac{\mathcal{P}_{J}^{(d)}\left(1+\tfrac{2t}{m^{2}}\right)}{\big{[}m^{2}(m^{2}+t)\big{]}^{k/2}}\right\rangle\qquad(t<0,\ k=2,4,\ldots)\,.} (21)

This simplification of eq.┬á(20) is only valid when neglecting EFT loops.

Let us record the first few two instances explicitly:

B2::subscript­ØÉÁ2absent\displaystyle B_{2}: 2ÔÇïg2ÔêÆg3ÔÇït+8ÔÇïg4ÔÇït2+ÔÇª2subscript­Øæö2subscript­Øæö3­Øæí8subscript­Øæö4superscript­Øæí2ÔÇª\displaystyle\quad 2g_{2}-g_{3}t+8g_{4}t^{2}+\ldots =Ôƒ¿(2ÔÇïm2+t)ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïtm2)m2ÔÇï(m2+t)2Ôƒ®,absentdelimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®2superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢12­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2superscript­ØæÜ2superscriptsuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæí2\displaystyle=\left\langle\frac{\left(2m^{2}+t\right)\mathcal{P}_{J}\left(1+\frac{2t}{m^{2}}\right)}{m^{2}\left(m^{2}+t\right)^{2}}\right\rangle\,, (22)
B4::subscript­ØÉÁ4absent\displaystyle B_{4}: 4ÔÇïg4+ÔÇª4subscript­Øæö4ÔÇª\displaystyle\quad 4g_{4}+\ldots =Ôƒ¿(2ÔÇïm2+t)ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïtm2)m4ÔÇï(m2+t)3Ôƒ®.absentdelimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®2superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢12­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2superscript­ØæÜ4superscriptsuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæí3\displaystyle=\left\langle\frac{\left(2m^{2}+t\right)\mathcal{P}_{J}\left(1+\frac{2t}{m^{2}}\right)}{m^{4}\left(m^{2}+t\right)^{3}}\right\rangle\,. (23)

The left-hand side has a regular series in t­Øæít, and the right-hand side involves Gegenbauers ­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïtm2)subscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢12­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2\mathcal{P}_{J}(1+\tfrac{2t}{m^{2}}), which can be straightforwardly expanded at small tÔë¬M2much-less-than­Øæísuperscript­ØæÇ2t\ll M^{2} using eq.┬á(9)ÔÇë. Recall that averages are taken over heavy states with mÔëÑM­ØæÜ­ØæÇm\geq M. Matching both sides order by order in t­Øæít generates a linear system in gnsubscript­Øæö­Øæøg_{n}ÔÇÖs:

g2subscript­Øæö2\displaystyle g_{2} =Ôƒ¿1m4Ôƒ®,g3=Ôƒ¿3ÔêÆ4dÔêÆ2ÔÇï­ØÆÑ2m6Ôƒ®,g4=Ôƒ¿12ÔÇïm8Ôƒ®,formulae-sequenceabsentdelimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®1superscript­ØæÜ4formulae-sequencesubscript­Øæö3delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®34­Øææ2superscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ6subscript­Øæö4delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®12superscript­ØæÜ8\displaystyle=\left\langle\frac{1}{m^{4}}\right\rangle\,,\qquad g_{3}=\left\langle\frac{3-\frac{4}{d-2}\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{6}}\right\rangle\,,\qquad g_{4}=\left\langle\frac{1}{2m^{8}}\right\rangle\,, (24)
g4subscript­Øæö4\displaystyle g_{4} =Ôƒ¿1+4ÔêÆ5ÔÇïd2ÔÇïdÔÇï(dÔêÆ2)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ2+1dÔÇï(dÔêÆ2)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ42ÔÇïm8Ôƒ®.absentdelimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®145­Øææ2­Øææ­Øææ2superscript­ØÆÑ21­Øææ­Øææ2superscript­ØÆÑ42superscript­ØæÜ8\displaystyle=\left\langle\frac{1+\frac{4-5d}{2d(d-2)}\mathcal{J}^{2}+\frac{1}{d(d-2)}\mathcal{J}^{4}}{2m^{8}}\right\rangle\,.

We introduced the spin Casimir ­ØÆÑ2=JÔÇï(J+dÔêÆ3)superscript­ØÆÑ2­ØÉ¢­ØÉ¢­Øææ3\mathcal{J}^{2}=J(J+d-3) for convenience. Note that we truncated Ôä│lowsubscriptÔä│low\mathcal{M}_{\text{low}} to order g4subscript­Øæö4g_{4}, but it is possible to work to higher orders and generate linear relations on couplings such as g5subscript­Øæö5g_{5} and so on.

The averaging notation immediately shows that g2,g4>0subscript­Øæö2subscript­Øæö40g_{2},g_{4}>0 since they are high-energy averages of positive quantities 1m41superscript­ØæÜ4\frac{1}{m^{4}} and 12ÔÇïm812superscript­ØæÜ8\frac{1}{2m^{8}}, respectively. Furthermore, the inequalities g3Ôëñ3ÔÇïg2M2subscript­Øæö33subscript­Øæö2superscript­ØæÇ2g_{3}\leq\frac{3g_{2}}{M^{2}} and g4Ôëñg22ÔÇïM4subscript­Øæö4subscript­Øæö22superscript­ØæÇ4g_{4}\leq\frac{g_{2}}{2M^{4}} also follow readily since mÔëÑM­ØæÜ­ØæÇm\geq M inside the average. In contrast, the sign of g3subscript­Øæö3g_{3} is not immediate due to the presence of spinning particles ÔÇô the magnitude of ­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØÆÑ2\mathcal{J}^{2} requires a deeper investigation. This difficulty was noted in attempted proofs of the six-dimensional a­ØæÄa-theorem Elvang:2012st .

The key to calculating a lower bound for g3subscript­Øæö3g_{3} will be the existence of two distinct averages that output g4subscript­Øæö4g_{4}. Equating them yields the first example of what turns out to be an infinite set of null constraints:

0=Ôƒ¿n4ÔÇï(m2,J)Ôƒ®,n4ÔÇï(m2,J)Ôëí­ØÆÑ2ÔÇï(2ÔÇï­ØÆÑ2ÔêÆ(5ÔÇïdÔêÆ4))m8.formulae-sequence0delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®subscript­Øæø4superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢subscript­Øæø4superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢superscript­ØÆÑ22superscript­ØÆÑ25­Øææ4superscript­ØæÜ80=\left\langle n_{4}(m^{2},J)\right\rangle,\qquad n_{4}(m^{2},J)\equiv\frac{\mathcal{J}^{2}\big{(}2\mathcal{J}^{2}-(5d-4)\big{)}}{m^{8}}\,. (25)

This is a constraint on the probabilities ¤üJÔÇï(s)subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢­Øæá\rho_{J}(s) which define the average Ôƒ¿ÔïàÔƒ®delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®Ôïà\left\langle\cdot\right\rangle. The subscript indicates the degree in 1/m21superscript­ØæÜ21/m^{2}. Physically this stems from crossing symmetry ÔÇô since there is a unique symmetric polynomial at degree 4, the coefficients of s2ÔÇït2superscript­Øæá2superscript­Øæí2s^{2}t^{2} and s4superscript­Øæá4s^{4} must be related. There are no lower-degree examples of this phenomenon: monomials with fewer than two powers of s­Øæás are killed by any double-subtracted sum rule, and odd powers of s­Øæás are information-free since fixed-t­Øæít dispersion relations preserve the sÔåöÔêÆsÔêÆtÔåö­Øæá­Øæá­Øæís\leftrightarrow-s-t symmetry of our problem.

Null constraints such as eq.┬á(25) will be central to this work. They balance spin-two states against higher spin states: as visible from fig.┬á5, the average vanishes for spin 0, is negative for spin 2, and positive for all other spins. This implies that, as soon as one particle of spin 2 is present, higher-spin particles must also be present, with predictable properties. (Spin two particles are singled out by the physical assumption that double-subtracted sum rule converges.)

3 Optimization framework

The Bksubscript­ØÉÁ­ØæÿB_{k} sum rules just introduced, coupled with positivity of high-energy averages Ôƒ¿ÔïàÔƒ®delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®Ôïà\left\langle\cdot\right\rangle provide a complete apparatus to establish potent self-consistency conditions on EFT coefficients gksubscript­Øæö­Øæÿg_{k}ÔÇÖs (defined in eq.┬á(5)). We recall our physical assumptions:

  • ÔÇó

    Double-subtracted dispersion relations converge

  • ÔÇó

    The low-energy amplitude is crossing symmetric

  • ÔÇó

    The high-energy spectral density is positive

Since we are considering averages over heavy states (with m>M­ØæÜ­ØæÇm>M), the coefficients (except in subsection 3.5) are naturally normalized by g2subscript­Øæö2g_{2} and the EFT cutoff M­ØæÇM. We will therefore be bounding dimensionless ratios:

g~3=g3ÔÇïM2g2,g~4=g4ÔÇïM4g2,g~5=g5ÔÇïM6g2,ÔÇªformulae-sequencesubscript~­Øæö3subscript­Øæö3superscript­ØæÇ2subscript­Øæö2formulae-sequencesubscript~­Øæö4subscript­Øæö4superscript­ØæÇ4subscript­Øæö2subscript~­Øæö5subscript­Øæö5superscript­ØæÇ6subscript­Øæö2ÔÇª\displaystyle\tilde{g}_{3}=g_{3}\frac{M^{2}}{g_{2}},\qquad\tilde{g}_{4}=g_{4}\frac{M^{4}}{g_{2}},\qquad\tilde{g}_{5}=g_{5}\frac{M^{6}}{g_{2}},\quad\dots (26)

Optimal bounds on these g~ksubscript~­Øæö­Øæÿ\tilde{g}_{k}ÔÇÖs will be found by formulating a dual problem, in which we combine the desired averages (such as 20) with null constraints (such as eq.┬á(25)) to obtain sign-definite sum rules. We first describe a simple example analytically, then describe a systematic implementation as a semi-definite problem amenable to publicly available software like SDPB Simmons-Duffin:2015qma .

3.1 Warm-up problem with three sum rules

As a warm-up, let us ask whether it is possible to lower-bound the g~3subscript~­Øæö3\tilde{g}_{3} coefficient using the B2,B4subscript­ØÉÁ2subscript­ØÉÁ4B_{2},B_{4} sum rules previously calculated. We consider the corresponding system of three equations from (24) (including the null constraint obtained via g4subscript­Øæö4g_{4} data):

g2=Ôƒ¿1m4Ôƒ®,g3=Ôƒ¿3ÔêÆ4dÔêÆ2ÔÇï­ØÆÑ2m6Ôƒ®,0=Ôƒ¿­ØÆÑ2ÔÇï(2ÔÇï­ØÆÑ2ÔêÆ5ÔÇïd+4)m8Ôƒ®.formulae-sequencesubscript­Øæö2delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®1superscript­ØæÜ4formulae-sequencesubscript­Øæö3delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®34­Øææ2superscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ60delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®superscript­ØÆÑ22superscript­ØÆÑ25­Øææ4superscript­ØæÜ8g_{2}=\left\langle\frac{1}{m^{4}}\right\rangle,\qquad g_{3}=\left\langle\frac{3-\frac{4}{d-2}\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{6}}\right\rangle,\qquad 0=\left\langle\frac{\mathcal{J}^{2}(2\mathcal{J}^{2}-5d+4)}{m^{8}}\right\rangle\,. (27)

With these definitions, let us examine a similar, but simpler set of relations:

h2=Ôƒ¿1m4Ôƒ®,h3=Ôƒ¿aÔêÆ­ØÆÑ2m6Ôƒ®,0=Ôƒ¿­ØÆÑ4ÔêÆbÔÇï­ØÆÑ2m8Ôƒ®ÔëíÔƒ¿nÔÇï(m2,J)Ôƒ®.formulae-sequencesubscriptÔäÄ2delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®1superscript­ØæÜ4formulae-sequencesubscriptÔäÄ3delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®­ØæÄsuperscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ60delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®superscript­ØÆÑ4­ØæÅsuperscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ8delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®­Øæøsuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢h_{2}=\left\langle\frac{1}{m^{4}}\right\rangle,\qquad h_{3}=\left\langle\frac{a-\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{6}}\right\rangle,\qquad 0=\left\langle\frac{\mathcal{J}^{4}-b\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{8}}\right\rangle\equiv\left\langle n(m^{2},J)\right\rangle\,. (28)

These relations take on the same form as original identities when a=3ÔÇï(dÔêÆ2)4­ØæÄ3­Øææ24a=\frac{3(d-2)}{4}, b=5ÔÇïdÔêÆ42­ØæÅ5­Øææ42b=\frac{5d-4}{2} and the coupling is rescaled to g3=4dÔêÆ2ÔÇïh3subscript­Øæö34­Øææ2subscriptÔäÄ3g_{3}=\frac{4}{d-2}h_{3}. Consequently, our warm-up problem is to lower-bound h3subscriptÔäÄ3h_{3}.

What makes a finite lower bound plausible is that the null constraint (the third equation) should somehow prevent large spins from contributing too much. This is an important point: the allowed range for g~3subscript~­Øæö3\tilde{g}_{3} is restricted by higher derivative crossing equations!

We now calculate a lower bound in two ways. The first ÔÇô and simplest ÔÇô method is to use the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality with the null constraint:

Ôƒ¿­ØÆÑ2m6Ôƒ®2ÔëñÔƒ¿1m4Ôƒ®ÔÇïÔƒ¿­ØÆÑ4m8Ôƒ®=bÔÇïÔƒ¿1m4Ôƒ®ÔÇïÔƒ¿­ØÆÑ2m8Ôƒ®.superscriptdelimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®superscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ62delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®1superscript­ØæÜ4delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®superscript­ØÆÑ4superscript­ØæÜ8­ØæÅdelimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®1superscript­ØæÜ4delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®superscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ8\left\langle\frac{\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{6}}\right\rangle^{2}\leq\left\langle\frac{1}{m^{4}}\right\rangle\left\langle\frac{\mathcal{J}^{4}}{m^{8}}\right\rangle=b\left\langle\frac{1}{m^{4}}\right\rangle\left\langle\frac{\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{8}}\right\rangle\,. (29)

Then, using the fact that m>M­ØæÜ­ØæÇm>M and ­ØÆÑ2ÔëÑ0superscript­ØÆÑ20\mathcal{J}^{2}\geq 0 inside the average yields Ôƒ¿­ØÆÑ2m8Ôƒ®Ôëñ1M2ÔÇïÔƒ¿­ØÆÑ2m6Ôƒ®delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®superscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ81superscript­ØæÇ2delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®superscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ6\left\langle\frac{\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{8}}\right\rangle\leq\frac{1}{M^{2}}\left\langle\frac{\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{6}}\right\rangle, and by dividing both sides by that average, we obtain an upper-bound on Ôƒ¿­ØÆÑ2m6Ôƒ®delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®superscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ6\left\langle\frac{\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{6}}\right\rangle as desired:

Ôƒ¿­ØÆÑ2m6Ôƒ®ÔëñbM2ÔÇïÔƒ¿1m4Ôƒ®ÔçÆh3ÔëÑÔêÆbM2ÔÇïh2.formulae-sequencedelimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®superscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ6­ØæÅsuperscript­ØæÇ2delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®1superscript­ØæÜ4ÔçÆsubscriptÔäÄ3­ØæÅsuperscript­ØæÇ2subscriptÔäÄ2\left\langle\frac{\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{6}}\right\rangle\leq\frac{b}{M^{2}}\left\langle\frac{1}{m^{4}}\right\rangle\quad\Rightarrow\quad h_{3}\geq-\frac{b}{M^{2}}h_{2}\,. (30)

This has a simple physical interpretation: if we define an impact parameter b~=2ÔÇïJm~­ØæÅ2­ØÉ¢­ØæÜ\tilde{b}=\frac{2J}{m}, then we have effectively shown that heavy states canÔÇÖt contribute at impact parameters much larger than Ôê╝1/Msimilar-toabsent1­ØæÇ\sim 1/M. In terms of the original problem (27), we have shown that

ÔêÆ2ÔÇï(5ÔÇïdÔêÆ4)dÔêÆ2Ôëñg3ÔÇïM2g2Ôëñ3.25­Øææ4­Øææ2subscript­Øæö3superscript­ØæÇ2subscript­Øæö23-\frac{2(5d-4)}{d-2}\leq\frac{g_{3}M^{2}}{g_{2}}\leq 3\,. (31)

This shows the existence of two-sided bounds for generic couplings. This is an important qualitative result, to our knowledge originally emphasized in talks : ratios of EFT couplings, in units of the cutoff scale M­ØæÇM, must be ­ØÆ¬ÔÇï(1)­ØÆ¬1\mathcal{O}(1) numbers. Numerically, however, the Cauchy-Schwarz method does not yield the optimal lower bound.

In contrast, the second ÔÇô and more powerful ÔÇô method is re-interpret the above task as a semi-definite problem, in order to systematically search for optimal bounds. Denote hiÔÇï(m2,J)subscriptÔäÄ­Øæûsuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢h_{i}(m^{2},J) the function whose average gives hisubscriptÔäÄ­Øæûh_{i}. The idea is to construct positive-definite combinations of the three averages in eq.┬á(28):

FÔÇï(m2,J)­ØÉ╣superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢\displaystyle F(m^{2},J) Ôëíh3ÔÇï(m2,J)+╬▒ÔÇïh2ÔÇï(m2,J)+╬▓ÔÇïnÔÇï(m2,J)absentsubscriptÔäÄ3superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢­Øø╝subscriptÔäÄ2superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢­Øø¢­Øæøsuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢\displaystyle\equiv h_{3}(m^{2},J)+\alpha h_{2}(m^{2},J)+\beta n(m^{2},J) (32)
=aÔêÆ­ØÆÑ2m6+╬▒m4+╬▓ÔÇï­ØÆÑ4ÔêÆbÔÇï­ØÆÑ2m8,absent­ØæÄsuperscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ6­Øø╝superscript­ØæÜ4­Øø¢superscript­ØÆÑ4­ØæÅsuperscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ8\displaystyle=\frac{a-\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{6}}+\frac{\alpha}{m^{4}}+\beta\frac{\mathcal{J}^{4}-b\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{8}}\,,

where we must find ╬▒,╬▓­Øø╝­Øø¢\alpha,\beta such that FÔÇï(m2,J)ÔëÑ0­ØÉ╣superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢0F(m^{2},J)\geq 0 for all J=0,2,4ÔÇïÔÇª­ØÉ¢024ÔÇªJ=0,2,4\ldots and mÔëÑM­ØæÜ­ØæÇm\geq M. Taking the average of any such F­ØÉ╣F then proves h3ÔëÑÔêÆ╬▒ÔÇïh2subscriptÔäÄ3­Øø╝subscriptÔäÄ2h_{3}\geq-\alpha h_{2}. The optimal bound will come from a non-negative F­ØÉ╣F with minimal ╬▒­Øø╝\alpha.

Let us first reproduce the first Cauchy-Schwarz argument in this language, which should give ╬▒=bM2­Øø╝­ØæÅsuperscript­ØæÇ2\alpha=\frac{b}{M^{2}}. Assume ╬▓>0­Øø¢0\beta>0. The argument amounts to completing squares in the ­ØÆÑ4/m8superscript­ØÆÑ4superscript­ØæÜ8\mathcal{J}^{4}/m^{8} term:

FÔÇï(m2,J)=╬▒ÔêÆ╬▓ÔÇï╬╗2m4+am6+­ØÆÑ2m6ÔÇï(2ÔÇï╬▓ÔÇï╬╗ÔêÆ1)ÔêÆ╬▓ÔÇïbÔÇï­ØÆÑ2m8+╬▓ÔÇï1m4ÔÇï(­ØÆÑ2m2ÔêÆ╬╗)2ÔÅƒpositive.­ØÉ╣superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢­Øø╝­Øø¢superscript­Ø£å2superscript­ØæÜ4­ØæÄsuperscript­ØæÜ6superscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ62­Øø¢­Ø£å1­Øø¢­ØæÅsuperscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ8subscriptÔÅƒ­Øø¢1superscript­ØæÜ4superscriptsuperscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ2­Ø£å2positiveF(m^{2},J)=\frac{\alpha-\beta\lambda^{2}}{m^{4}}+\frac{a}{m^{6}}+\frac{\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{6}}\left(2\beta\lambda-1\right)-\beta b\frac{\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{8}}+\underbrace{\beta\frac{1}{m^{4}}\left(\frac{\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{2}}-\lambda\right)^{2}}_{\rm positive}\,. (33)

For any ╬╗­Ø£å\lambda this is an identical rewriting of eq.┬á(32), and the Cauchy-Schwartz-like method is to choose ╬╗,╬▓­Ø£å­Øø¢\lambda,\beta such that the other terms are positive as well. From the limit ­ØÆÑÔåÆÔê×ÔåÆ­ØÆÑ\mathcal{J}\to\infty, the terms with ­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØÆÑ2\mathcal{J}^{2} need to give a positive functions of m­ØæÜm, which imposes that (2ÔÇï╬▓ÔÇï╬╗ÔêÆ1ÔêÆ╬▓ÔÇïb/M2)ÔëÑ02­Øø¢­Ø£å1­Øø¢­ØæÅsuperscript­ØæÇ20(2\beta\lambda-1-\beta b/M^{2})\geq 0. To minimize ╬▒­Øø╝\alpha we must minimize ╬▓ÔÇï╬╗2­Øø¢superscript­Ø£å2\beta\lambda^{2}; we find that the minimum saturates the inequality, and is simply ╬▓=M2/b­Øø¢superscript­ØæÇ2­ØæÅ\beta=M^{2}/b with ╬▓ÔÇï╬╗=1­Øø¢­Ø£å1\beta\lambda=1. With this choice, our trial functional becomes

FÔÇï(m2,J)=╬▒ÔêÆbM2m4+am6+­ØÆÑ2m6ÔÇï(1M2ÔêÆ1m2)+M2bÔÇïm4ÔÇï(­ØÆÑ2m2ÔêÆbM2)2ÔÅƒpositive.­ØÉ╣superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢­Øø╝­ØæÅsuperscript­ØæÇ2superscript­ØæÜ4­ØæÄsuperscript­ØæÜ6subscriptÔÅƒsuperscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ61superscript­ØæÇ21superscript­ØæÜ2superscript­ØæÇ2­ØæÅsuperscript­ØæÜ4superscriptsuperscript­ØÆÑ2superscript­ØæÜ2­ØæÅsuperscript­ØæÇ22positiveF(m^{2},J)=\frac{\alpha-\frac{b}{M^{2}}}{m^{4}}+\frac{a}{m^{6}}+\underbrace{\frac{\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{6}}\left(\frac{1}{M^{2}}-\frac{1}{m^{2}}\right)+\frac{M^{2}}{bm^{4}}\left(\frac{\mathcal{J}^{2}}{m^{2}}-\frac{b}{M^{2}}\right)^{2}}_{\rm positive}\,. (34)

The minimal ╬▒­Øø╝\alpha for which the first terms are positive for all m2superscript­ØæÜ2m^{2} is then ╬▒=bM2­Øø╝­ØæÅsuperscript­ØæÇ2\alpha=\frac{b}{M^{2}}, precisely as anticipated! We have thus exhibited a positive functional F­ØÉ╣F which proves the Cauchy-Schwarz bound in (30).

It is now easy to see why this bound is not optimal: F­ØÉ╣F doesnÔÇÖt need to be expressible as a sum of three separately positive parts!

In d=4­Øææ4d=4, for example, the above argument gives ÔêÆ16Ôëñg~316subscript~­Øæö3-16\leq\tilde{g}_{3}. In comparison, using the numerical search strategy detailed in the next subsections, we find that the following combination is positive for all J=0,2,4ÔÇïÔÇª­ØÉ¢024ÔÇªJ=0,2,4\ldots and mÔëÑM­ØæÜ­ØæÇm\geq M:

0ÔëñFÔÇï(m2,J)=(10.6124910.0671875)Ôïà(g2ÔÇï(m2,J)M2ÔÇïg3ÔÇï(m2,J)M4ÔÇïn4ÔÇï(m2,J)).0­ØÉ╣superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢Ôïàmatrix10.6124910.0671875matrixsubscript­Øæö2superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢superscript­ØæÇ2subscript­Øæö3superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢superscript­ØæÇ4subscript­Øæø4superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢\displaystyle 0\leq F(m^{2},J)=\begin{pmatrix}10.61249\\ 1\\ 0.0671875\end{pmatrix}\cdot\begin{pmatrix}g_{2}(m^{2},J)\\ M^{2}g_{3}(m^{2},J)\\ M^{4}n_{4}(m^{2},J)\end{pmatrix}. (35)

This allows to infer, by taking the average of this inequality, that

ÔêÆ10.61249Ôëñg~3.10.61249subscript~­Øæö3\displaystyle-10.61249\leq\tilde{g}_{3}\,. (36)

This is significantly stronger than ÔêÆ16Ôëñg~316subscript~­Øæö3-16\leq\tilde{g}_{3} that we just derived in an ad hoc manner. How can we understand the solution (35) analytically? They key ingredient will be that spins J­ØÉ¢J are discrete, whereas our ad hoc bound treated J­ØÉ¢JÔÇÖs as continuous parameter.

We now calculate this bound analytically. Let us return to the functional ansatz (32) and try to directly constrain the unknowns ╬▒­Øø╝\alpha, ╬▓­Øø¢\beta. Putting J=0­ØÉ¢0J=0 we only deduce ╬▒ÔëÑ0­Øø╝0\alpha\geq 0. Putting J=2,4ÔÇïÔÇª­ØÉ¢24ÔÇªJ=2,4\ldots gives a sequence of quadratic polynomials in (m2ÔêÆM2)superscript­ØæÜ2superscript­ØæÇ2(m^{2}-M^{2}), each with positive curvature. Such polynomials are non-negative if the two roots are negative, or if both roots are positive and equal, or if they are complex conjugate pairs. It seems virtually impossible to guess a priori which case is realized, however this information is readily gleaned by plotting the numerical polynomials (35), as shown in fig.┬á6. We see that the J=2,4­ØÉ¢24J=2,4 inequalities are both saturated: the former by having a root at m2=M2superscript­ØæÜ2superscript­ØæÇ2m^{2}=M^{2}, and the latter by having a positive double root (i.e. vanishing discriminant).

These two saturated inequalities give algebraic equations that may be solved analytically; this determines the vector (╬▒Ôêù,1,╬▓Ôêù)subscript­Øø╝1subscript­Øø¢(\alpha_{*},1,\beta_{*}) in eq.┬á(35) to be:

╬▒Ôêù=5ÔÇïdÔêÆ2+(d+3)ÔÇï(319ÔÇïd3+76ÔÇïd2ÔêÆ292ÔÇïd+32)6ÔÇï(d+1)ÔÇï(d+4)2ÔÇï(dÔêÆ2),╬▓Ôêù=12ÔÇïdÔÇï(dÔêÆ1)ÔÇï(╬▒ÔêùÔêÆ5ÔÇïdÔêÆ2dÔêÆ2).formulae-sequencesubscript­Øø╝5­Øææ2­Øææ3319superscript­Øææ376superscript­Øææ2292­Øææ326­Øææ1­Øææ42­Øææ2subscript­Øø¢12­Øææ­Øææ1subscript­Øø╝5­Øææ2­Øææ2\alpha_{*}=\frac{5d-2+\sqrt{\frac{(d+3)(319d^{3}+76d^{2}-292d+32)}{6(d+1)(d+4)}}}{2(d-2)},\quad\beta_{*}=\frac{1}{2d(d-1)}\left(\alpha_{*}-\frac{5d-2}{d-2}\right)\,. (37)

In d=4­Øææ4d=4, this gives ╬▒Ôêù=92+74ÔÇï615Ôëê10.61249ÔÇïÔÇªsubscript­Øø╝927461510.61249ÔÇª\alpha_{*}=\frac{9}{2}+\frac{7}{4}\sqrt{\frac{61}{5}}\approx 10.61249\ldots precisely as found numerically! This result showcases the use of numerics to guide our analytical understanding. Moreover, the preceding formula can be shown to give the correct optimal lower bound on g~3subscript~­Øæö3\tilde{g}_{3} in any dÔëÑ3­Øææ3d\geq 3, when including the single null constraint n4subscript­Øæø4n_{4}. (When we add more equations below, the d=4­Øææ4d=4 bound will converge to g~3ÔëÑÔêÆ10.346subscript~­Øæö310.346\tilde{g}_{3}\geq-10.346.)

3.2 Dual problem: general formulation

We now introduce the general ÔÇ£dualÔÇØ optimization problem which allows to carve out the space of EFT coefficients g~3,g~4,g~5,ÔÇªsubscript~­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö4subscript~­Øæö5ÔÇª\tilde{g}_{3},\tilde{g}_{4},\tilde{g}_{5},\dots allowed by unitarity and positivity.

The data at our disposal comes from the Bksubscript­ØÉÁ­ØæÿB_{k} sum-rules in eq.┬á(21):

  1. 1.

    Representative averages gkÔÇï(m2,J)subscript­Øæö­Øæÿsuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢g_{k}(m^{2},J) which measure each ÔÇ£desiredÔÇØ gksubscript­Øæö­Øæÿg_{k}:

    g2=Ôƒ¿g2ÔÇï(m2,J)Ôƒ®,g3=Ôƒ¿g3ÔÇï(m2,J)Ôƒ®,g4=Ôƒ¿g4ÔÇï(m2,J)Ôƒ®,Ôï»formulae-sequencesubscript­Øæö2delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®subscript­Øæö2superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢formulae-sequencesubscript­Øæö3delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®subscript­Øæö3superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢subscript­Øæö4delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®subscript­Øæö4superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢Ôï»\displaystyle g_{2}=\left\langle g_{2}(m^{2},J)\right\rangle,\quad g_{3}=\left\langle g_{3}(m^{2},J)\right\rangle,\quad g_{4}=\left\langle g_{4}(m^{2},J)\right\rangle,\quad\cdots (38)
  2. 2.

    A set of null functions niÔÇï(m2,J)subscript­Øæø­Øæûsuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢n_{i}(m^{2},J) whose heavy averages vanish:

    0=Ôƒ¿n4ÔÇï(m2,J)Ôƒ®=Ôƒ¿n5ÔÇï(m2,J)Ôƒ®=Ôƒ¿n6ÔÇï(m2,J)Ôƒ®=ÔÇª0delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®subscript­Øæø4superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®subscript­Øæø5superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®subscript­Øæø6superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢ÔÇª\displaystyle 0=\left\langle n_{4}(m^{2},J)\right\rangle=\left\langle n_{5}(m^{2},J)\right\rangle=\left\langle n_{6}(m^{2},J)\right\rangle=\dots (39)

First consider the problem of lower-bounding g3subscript­Øæö3g_{3}, given g2subscript­Øæö2g_{2}, but being agnostic about the other gksubscript­Øæö­Øæÿg_{k}ÔÇÖs with kÔëÑ4­Øæÿ4k\geq 4. Define a vector of functions which combines g2,g3subscript­Øæö2subscript­Øæö3g_{2},g_{3} and null constraints:

vÔÇï(m2,J)Ôëí(g2ÔÇï(m2,J),M2ÔÇïg3ÔÇï(m2,J),n4ÔÇï(m2,J),n5ÔÇï(m2,J),ÔÇª).­Øæúsuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢subscript­Øæö2superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢superscript­ØæÇ2subscript­Øæö3superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢subscript­Øæø4superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢subscript­Øæø5superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢ÔÇªv(m^{2},J)\equiv\big{(}g_{2}(m^{2},J),\ M^{2}g_{3}(m^{2},J),\ n_{4}(m^{2},J),\ n_{5}(m^{2},J),\ \ldots\big{)}\,. (40)

The following (ÔÇ£dualÔÇØ) optimization problem then determines a lower bound on g3subscript­Øæö3g_{3}:

{maximize:Asubject to:0Ôëñ(ÔêÆA,1,c4,c5,ÔÇª)ÔïàvÔÇï(m2,J)ÔêÇmÔëÑM,ÔêÇJ=0,2,4,ÔÇªcasesmaximize:­ØÉ┤subject to:formulae-sequence0Ôïà­ØÉ┤1subscript­ØæÉ4subscript­ØæÉ5ÔÇª­Øæúsuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢formulae-sequencefor-all­ØæÜ­ØæÇfor-all­ØÉ¢024ÔÇª\displaystyle\left\{\begin{array}[]{ll}\displaystyle\text{maximize:}&\quad A\\ \displaystyle\text{subject to:}&\quad 0\leq\big{(}{-}A,1,c_{4},c_{5},\ldots\big{)}\cdot v(m^{2},J)\qquad\forall\ m\geq M,\ \forall J=0,2,4,\ldots\end{array}\right. (43)

Here we are maximizing over A­ØÉ┤A and all possible linear combinations cisubscript­ØæÉ­Øæûc_{i} of the null constraints niÔÇï(m2,J)subscript­Øæø­Øæûsuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢n_{i}(m^{2},J). Having found such a linear combination cisubscript­ØæÉ­Øæûc_{i} and an optimal number A­ØÉ┤A, it readily follows from linearity and positivity of Ôƒ¿ÔïàÔƒ®delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®Ôïà\left\langle\cdot\right\rangle that

0ÔëñÔêÆAÔÇïg2+M2ÔÇïg3+0,0­ØÉ┤subscript­Øæö2superscript­ØæÇ2subscript­Øæö30\displaystyle 0\leq-Ag_{2}+M^{2}g_{3}+0, (44)

allowing the desired optimal bound on g~3subscript~­Øæö3\tilde{g}_{3} to be inferred:

AÔëñg~3.­ØÉ┤subscript~­Øæö3\displaystyle A\leq\tilde{g}_{3}. (45)

This equation motivates the maximization of A­ØÉ┤A. Alternatively, to find an optimal upper bound B­ØÉÁB, it is enough to consider the analogous problem:

{minimize:Bsubject to:0Ôëñ(B,ÔêÆ1,c4,c5,ÔÇª)ÔïàvÔÇï(m2,J)ÔêÇmÔëÑM,ÔêÇJ=0,2,4,ÔÇªcasesminimize:­ØÉÁsubject to:formulae-sequence0Ôïà­ØÉÁ1subscript­ØæÉ4subscript­ØæÉ5ÔÇª­Øæúsuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢formulae-sequencefor-all­ØæÜ­ØæÇfor-all­ØÉ¢024ÔÇª\displaystyle\left\{\begin{array}[]{ll}\displaystyle\text{minimize:}&\quad B\\ \displaystyle\text{subject to:}&\quad 0\leq\big{(}B,{-}1,c_{4},c_{5},\ldots\big{)}\cdot v(m^{2},J)\qquad\forall\ m\geq M,\ \forall J=0,2,4,\ldots\end{array}\right. (48)

It then follows that g~3ÔëñBsubscript~­Øæö3­ØÉÁ\tilde{g}_{3}\leq B. Taking the intersection of these two sets yields a convex region defined by

AÔëñg~3ÔëñB.­ØÉ┤subscript~­Øæö3­ØÉÁ\displaystyle A\leq\tilde{g}_{3}\leq B. (49)

These are the inequalities inferred on g~3subscript~­Øæö3\tilde{g}_{3} from data in the Bksubscript­ØÉÁ­ØæÿB_{k} sum-rules.

In theory, the null constraints nisubscript­Øæø­Øæûn_{i} are part of an infinite dimensional vector space, but for numerical purposes, the dimensionality is taken to be finite and determined by the truncation order of the low-energy expansion Ôä│lowsubscriptÔä│low\mathcal{M}_{\text{low}}. We will find that the optimal bounds converge rapidly as the maximal degree is increased.

This problem can be readily adapted if we are given additional assumptions. For example, to make exclusion plots in the (g~3,g~4)subscript~­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö4(\tilde{g}_{3},\tilde{g}_{4}) plane, one strategy is to postulate some value of g~3(0)superscriptsubscript~­Øæö30\tilde{g}_{3}^{(0)} in the allowed range, and for each value we determine lower and upper bounds on g~4subscript~­Øæö4\tilde{g}_{4}. The vector v­Øæúv in eq.┬á(40) then acquires an extra g4subscript­Øæö4g_{4} row:

vÔåª(g2,M2ÔÇïg3,M4ÔÇïg4,n4,n5,ÔÇª).maps-to­Øæúsubscript­Øæö2superscript­ØæÇ2subscript­Øæö3superscript­ØæÇ4subscript­Øæö4subscript­Øæø4subscript­Øæø5ÔÇªv\mapsto\big{(}g_{2},\ M^{2}g_{3},\ M^{4}g_{4},\ n_{4},\ n_{5},\ \ldots\big{)}\,. (50)

Imposing positivity of (ÔêÆA,ÔêÆB,1,c4,c5,ÔÇª)ÔïàvÔÇï(m,J)Ôïà­ØÉ┤­ØÉÁ1subscript­ØæÉ4subscript­ØæÉ5ÔÇª­Øæú­ØæÜ­ØÉ¢\big{(}{-}A,{-}B,1,c_{4},c_{5},\ldots\big{)}\cdot v(m,J) for all m­ØæÜm and J­ØÉ¢J yields a lower bound g~4ÔëÑA+BÔÇïg~3(0)subscript~­Øæö4­ØÉ┤­ØÉÁsubscriptsuperscript~­Øæö03\tilde{g}_{4}\geq A+B\tilde{g}^{(0)}_{3}, for example. In fact, since the EFT parameters enter linearly, the resulting inequality automatically carves out a half-space in the (g~3,g~4)subscript~­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö4(\tilde{g}_{3},\tilde{g}_{4})-plane:

g~4ÔêÆBÔÇïg~3ÔëÑA.subscript~­Øæö4­ØÉÁsubscript~­Øæö3­ØÉ┤\tilde{g}_{4}-B\tilde{g}_{3}\geq A. (51)

This half-plane is tangent to the allowed region at g~3=g~3(0)subscript~­Øæö3superscriptsubscript~­Øæö30\tilde{g}_{3}=\tilde{g}_{3}^{(0)}. If this process is repeated for distinct g~3(0)superscriptsubscript~­Øæö30\tilde{g}_{3}^{(0)}, a collection of planes is rapidly generated from which it is possible to carve out the convex allowed space.

This processes generalizes to higher dimensional planes (i.e. hyperplanes): the vector v­Øæúv always contains only those coefficients we are not being agnostic about, plus an arbitrary number of null constraints. Although we will not go beyond three-dimensional regions, we note that an efficient search algorithm in higher dimensions is described in ref.┬áChester:2019ifh .

3.3 Example with a SDPB implementation

The optimization problem just formulated is in a form that is directly amenable to the SDPB solver Simmons-Duffin:2015qma . There are just two simple substitutions to make:

  1. 1.

    The program accepts polynomials of xÔëÑ0­ØæÑ0x\geq 0; we set m2ÔåªM2ÔÇï(1+x)maps-tosuperscript­ØæÜ2superscript­ØæÇ21­ØæÑm^{2}\mapsto M^{2}(1+x) and remove a common denominator.

  2. 2.

    The program accepts finite lists of polynomial constraints. We tabulate a finite list of spins J=0,2,ÔÇª,Jmax­ØÉ¢02ÔÇªsubscript­ØÉ¢maxJ=0,2,\dots,J_{\text{max}} and add a single function of x­ØæÑx corresponding to JÔåÆÔê×ÔåÆ­ØÉ¢J\to\infty.

The second truncation is valid as long as Jmaxsubscript­ØÉ¢maxJ_{\text{max}} is taken sufficiently large; once convergence is achieved, further increases of Jmaxsubscript­ØÉ¢maxJ_{\rm max} have no effect on the bounds.

We consider now an example relevant to one of the plots in the next section, when working in d=4­Øææ4d=4, Jmax=40subscript­ØÉ¢max40J_{\text{max}}=40 and Mandelstam order n=4­Øæø4n=4. The goal is to find a lower bounding plane on g~4subscript~­Øæö4\tilde{g}_{4} for fixed g~3subscript~­Øæö3\tilde{g}_{3}, say g~3=ÔêÆ10.5subscript~­Øæö310.5\tilde{g}_{3}=-10.5 (which is slightly above the allowed lower bound (35) for this truncation). The polynomial vector v­Øæúv combines the three observed coefficients and a single null constraint n4subscript­Øæø4n_{4}, rescaled by appropriate powers of M­ØæÇM:

vÔÇï(x,J)­Øæú­ØæÑ­ØÉ¢\displaystyle v(x,J) Ôëí\displaystyle\equiv M4ÔÇï(1+x)4├ù(g2,M2ÔÇïg3,M4ÔÇïg4,M4ÔÇïn4)superscript­ØæÇ4superscript1­ØæÑ4subscript­Øæö2superscript­ØæÇ2subscript­Øæö3superscript­ØæÇ4subscript­Øæö4superscript­ØæÇ4subscript­Øæø4\displaystyle M^{4}(1+x)^{4}\times\left(g_{2},M^{2}g_{3},M^{4}g_{4},M^{4}n_{4}\right) (52)
=\displaystyle= ((1+x)2,(1+x)ÔÇï(3ÔêÆ2ÔÇïJÔÇï(J+1)),12,2ÔÇïJÔÇï(J+1)ÔÇï(JÔÇï(J+1)ÔêÆ8)).superscript1­ØæÑ21­ØæÑ32­ØÉ¢­ØÉ¢1122­ØÉ¢­ØÉ¢1­ØÉ¢­ØÉ¢18\displaystyle\left((1+x)^{2},(1+x)(3-2J(J+1)),\ \tfrac{1}{2},2J(J+1)(J(J+1)-8)\right). (53)

In addition to tabulating v­Øæúv for all even JÔëñJmax­ØÉ¢subscript­ØÉ¢maxJ\leq J_{\rm max}, we also include the infinite-J­ØÉ¢J limit, which is simply the coefficient of J4superscript­ØÉ¢4J^{4}:

vÔÇï(x,Ôê×)=(0, 0, 0, 2).­Øæú­ØæÑ0 0 02v(x,\infty)=\left(0,\ 0,\ 0,\ 2\right)\,. (54)

To lower-bound g~4subscript~­Øæö4\tilde{g}_{4} at the stated value of g~3subscript~­Øæö3\tilde{g}_{3}, we search for four-vectors y­Øæªy, normalized to yÔïà(0,0,1,0)=1Ôïà­Øæª00101y{\cdot}\big{(}0,0,1,0\big{)}=1, which solve the following problem:

{maximize:h=yÔïà(ÔêÆ1,ÔêÆ(ÔêÆ10.5),0,0)subject to:0ÔëñyÔïàvÔÇï(x,J)ÔêÇx>0,JÔêê{0,2,Ôï»,40,Ôê×}.casesmaximize:ÔäÄÔïà­Øæª110.500subject to:formulae-sequence0Ôïà­Øæª­Øæú­ØæÑ­ØÉ¢formulae-sequencefor-all­ØæÑ0­ØÉ¢02Ôï»40\displaystyle\left\{\begin{array}[]{ll}\displaystyle\text{maximize:}&\quad h=y{\cdot}\left(-1,-(-10.5),0,0\right)\\ \displaystyle\text{subject to:}&\quad 0\leq y{\cdot}v(x,J)\qquad\forall\ x>0,J\in\{0,2,\cdots,40,\infty\}\,.\end{array}\right. (57)

The lower bound is then

g~4ÔëÑh(at┬áÔÇïg~3=ÔêÆ10.5).subscript~­Øæö4ÔäÄat┬ásubscript~­Øæö310.5\tilde{g}_{4}\geq h\qquad(\mbox{at }\tilde{g}_{3}=-10.5)\,. (58)

The solution vector y­Øæªy computed by SDPB was found to be yÔëê(1.4823, 0.1810, 1, 0.01472)­Øæª1.48230.181010.01472y\approx\left(1.4823,\ 0.1810,\ 1,\ 0.01472\right), giving h=0.4183ÔäÄ0.4183h=0.4183. The half-plane allowed by positivity of yÔïàvÔïà­Øæª­Øæúy{\cdot}v is thus

g~4+0.1810ÔÇï(g~3+10.5)ÔëÑ0.4183subscript~­Øæö40.1810subscript~­Øæö310.50.4183\tilde{g}_{4}+0.1810(\tilde{g}_{3}+10.5)\geq 0.4183 (59)

which gives one of the boundaries used to make the n=4­Øæø4n=4 region in fig.┬á10 (b) below.

3.4 Generating null constraints

So far we used a single constraint from crossing: the null average n4ÔÇï(m2,J)subscript­Øæø4superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢n_{4}(m^{2},J) from eq.┬á(25). The bounds improve after we add more constraints. Let us describe a way to generate them.

A straightforward method is as follows. First, fix a degree in Mandelstam invariants, n­Øæøn. Then, list all the Ôîèn2Ôîï­Øæø2\left\lfloor\tfrac{n}{2}\right\rfloor low-energy averages corresponding to this degree, namely the coefficient of tnÔêÆksuperscript­Øæí­Øæø­Øæÿt^{n-k} in the left-hand-side of the BkÔÇï(t)subscript­ØÉÁ­Øæÿ­ØæíB_{k}(t) sum rules (21) with kÔëñn­Øæÿ­Øæøk\leq n, using the crossing-symmetric low-energy ansatz (5). The right-hand-sides of those linear combinations with vanishing left-hand-side then constitute a basis of null constraints.

The first few cases, up to degree n=7­Øæø7n=7, are (in arbitrary normalization):

m8ÔÇïn4=superscript­ØæÜ8subscript­Øæø4absent\displaystyle m^{8}n_{4}= (4ÔêÆ5ÔÇïd)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ2+2ÔÇï­ØÆÑ4,45­Øææsuperscript­ØÆÑ22superscript­ØÆÑ4\displaystyle\ (4-5d)\mathcal{J}^{2}+2\mathcal{J}^{4}\,, (60)
m10ÔÇïn5=superscript­ØæÜ10subscript­Øæø5absent\displaystyle m^{10}n_{5}= (23ÔÇïd2ÔêÆ12ÔÇïdÔêÆ20)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ2+(ÔêÆ21ÔÇïdÔêÆ2)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ4+4ÔÇï­ØÆÑ6,23superscript­Øææ212­Øææ20superscript­ØÆÑ221­Øææ2superscript­ØÆÑ44superscript­ØÆÑ6\displaystyle\left(23d^{2}-12d-20\right)\mathcal{J}^{2}+(-21d-2)\mathcal{J}^{4}+4\mathcal{J}^{6}\,,
m12ÔÇïn6=superscript­ØæÜ12subscript­Øæø6absent\displaystyle m^{12}n_{6}= 4ÔÇï(13ÔÇïd2+21ÔÇïd+2)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ4ÔêÆ3ÔÇï(d+2)ÔÇï(17ÔÇïd2+4ÔÇïdÔêÆ16)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ2+2ÔÇï(ÔêÆ9ÔÇïdÔêÆ8)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ6+2ÔÇï­ØÆÑ8,413superscript­Øææ221­Øææ2superscript­ØÆÑ43­Øææ217superscript­Øææ24­Øææ16superscript­ØÆÑ229­Øææ8superscript­ØÆÑ62superscript­ØÆÑ8\displaystyle\ 4\left(13d^{2}+21d+2\right)\mathcal{J}^{4}-3(d+2)\left(17d^{2}+4d-16\right)\mathcal{J}^{2}+2(-9d-8)\mathcal{J}^{6}+2\mathcal{J}^{8}\,,
m14ÔÇïn7=superscript­ØæÜ14subscript­Øæø7absent\displaystyle m^{14}n_{7}= 6ÔÇï(45ÔÇïd2+140ÔÇïd+92)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ6+4ÔÇï(ÔêÆ140ÔÇïd3ÔêÆ619ÔÇïd2ÔêÆ711ÔÇïdÔêÆ46)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ4645superscript­Øææ2140­Øææ92superscript­ØÆÑ64140superscript­Øææ3619superscript­Øææ2711­Øææ46superscript­ØÆÑ4\displaystyle\ 6\left(45d^{2}+140d+92\right)\mathcal{J}^{6}+4\left(-140d^{3}-619d^{2}-711d-46\right)\mathcal{J}^{4}
+(401ÔÇïd4+2108ÔÇïd3+2284ÔÇïd2ÔêÆ3008ÔÇïdÔêÆ3360)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ2+5ÔÇï(ÔêÆ11ÔÇïdÔêÆ18)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ8+4ÔÇï­ØÆÑ10,401superscript­Øææ42108superscript­Øææ32284superscript­Øææ23008­Øææ3360superscript­ØÆÑ2511­Øææ18superscript­ØÆÑ84superscript­ØÆÑ10\displaystyle+\left(401d^{4}+2108d^{3}+2284d^{2}-3008d-3360\right)\mathcal{J}^{2}+5(-11d-18)\mathcal{J}^{8}+4\mathcal{J}^{10}\,,
m14ÔÇïn7ÔÇ▓=superscript­ØæÜ14superscriptsubscript­Øæø7ÔÇ▓absent\displaystyle m^{14}n_{7}^{\prime}= (ÔêÆ27ÔÇïdÔêÆ14)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ4+2ÔÇï(dÔêÆ1)ÔÇï(19ÔÇïd+22)ÔÇï­ØÆÑ2+4ÔÇï­ØÆÑ627­Øææ14superscript­ØÆÑ42­Øææ119­Øææ22superscript­ØÆÑ24superscript­ØÆÑ6\displaystyle\left(-27d-14\right)\mathcal{J}^{4}+2(d-1)(19d+22)\mathcal{J}^{2}+4\mathcal{J}^{6}

where we recall that ­ØÆÑ2=JÔÇï(J+dÔêÆ3)superscript­ØÆÑ2­ØÉ¢­ØÉ¢­Øææ3\mathcal{J}^{2}=J(J+d-3). Note that all null averages vanish when J=0­ØÉ¢0J=0: they relate spinning heavy states to one another, but spinless states are completely decoupled.

We find that there exists a single null constraint for each degree n=4,5,6­Øæø456n=4,5,6, then two constraint each for n=7,8,9­Øæø789n=7,8,9, three each for n=10,11,12­Øæø101112n=10,11,12, etc.: the number of linearly independent null constraints at each degree increases by 1 for every increase of n­Øæøn by 3. A sequence of generating functions XkÔÇï(t)subscript­Øæï­Øæÿ­ØæíX_{k}(t) which enumerates them all is discussed in appendix A.

Finally, it is important to stress that the null constraints only average to zero modulo EFT loops, since the method for finding them relies on the explicit tree-level parameterization (5). The interpretation of resulting inequalities as bounds on gksubscript­Øæö­Øæÿg_{k} is thus only strictly valid in this approximation. In an interacting EFT, the coefficients gksubscript­Øæö­Øæÿg_{k} depend on choices of scale and renormalization scheme, and the correct interpretation of the positive functionals F­ØÉ╣F is that they give rigorous (possibly non-optimal) inequalities of the form:

Ôƒ¿g3ÔÇï(m2,J)+ÔêæiciÔÇïniÔÇï(m2,J)Ôƒ®ÔëÑAÔÇïÔƒ¿g2ÔÇï(m2,J)Ôƒ®delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®subscript­Øæö3superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢subscript­Øæûsubscript­ØæÉ­Øæûsubscript­Øæø­Øæûsuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢­ØÉ┤delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®subscript­Øæö2superscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢\left\langle g_{3}(m^{2},J)+\sum_{i}c_{i}n_{i}(m^{2},J)\right\rangle\geq A\left\langle g_{2}(m^{2},J)\right\rangle (61)

where all averages are computed as integrals over arcs with |s|ÔëêM2­Øæásuperscript­ØæÇ2|s|\approx M^{2} following eq.┬á(20). The method thus produces rigorous bounds on computable combinations of EFT couplings at the scale M­ØæÇM.

3.5 An ad hoc upper bound on (Ôêé¤ò)4superscriptitalic-¤ò4(\partial\phi)^{4}

The systematic method explained above bounds ratios gk/g2subscript­Øæö­Øæÿsubscript­Øæö2g_{k}/g_{2}, but how about g2subscript­Øæö2g_{2} itself? Here we present one upper bound on g2subscript­Øæö2g_{2}; this subsection is somewhat separate from the rest since we were unable to systematically optimize the bound.

The naive intuition is that if heavy states couplings are order unity, then g2Ôê╝1Mdsimilar-tosubscript­Øæö21superscript­ØæÇ­Øææg_{2}\sim\frac{1}{M^{d}}. An upper bound with this scaling should thus follow from the unitarity limit, ¤üJÔëñ2subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢2\rho_{J}\leq 2. This would be the full story if heavy states only had a finite number of spins, however, to get an actual bound one must also control the infinite sum over spins. The idea is to combine the sum rule for g2subscript­Øæö2g_{2} with a multiple of the first null constraint n4subscript­Øæø4n_{4}:

g2=Ôƒ¿1m4ÔêÆ╬▒ÔÇïfÔÇï(J)m8Ôƒ®withfÔÇï(J)=­ØÆÑ2ÔÇï(2ÔÇï­ØÆÑ2ÔêÆ(5ÔÇïdÔêÆ4)).formulae-sequencesubscript­Øæö2delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®1superscript­ØæÜ4­Øø╝­Øæô­ØÉ¢superscript­ØæÜ8with­Øæô­ØÉ¢superscript­ØÆÑ22superscript­ØÆÑ25­Øææ4g_{2}=\left\langle\frac{1}{m^{4}}-\alpha\frac{f(J)}{m^{8}}\right\rangle\quad\mbox{with}\quad f(J)=\mathcal{J}^{2}\big{(}2\mathcal{J}^{2}-(5d-4)\big{)}\,. (62)

This holds for any ╬▒­Øø╝\alpha; we recall that ­ØÆÑ2=JÔÇï(J+dÔêÆ3)superscript­ØÆÑ2­ØÉ¢­ØÉ¢­Øææ3\mathcal{J}^{2}=J(J+d-3) is the spin Casimir. Inserting the definition (17) of the average and switching to a dimensionless mass parameter z=M2/m2<1­Øæºsuperscript­ØæÇ2superscript­ØæÜ21z=M^{2}/m^{2}<1, this can be rewritten

g2=1¤ÇÔÇïMdÔÇïÔêæJÔÇï┬áeven┬ánJ(d)ÔÇïÔê½01dÔÇïzzÔÇïzd2ÔÇï¤üJÔÇï(M2/z)ÔÇï(1ÔêÆ╬▒ÔÇïz2ÔÇïfÔÇï(J)).subscript­Øæö21­Ø£ïsuperscript­ØæÇ­Øææsubscript­ØÉ¢┬áeven┬ásuperscriptsubscript­Øæø­ØÉ¢­Øææsuperscriptsubscript01­Øææ­Øæº­Øæºsuperscript­Øæº­Øææ2subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢superscript­ØæÇ2­Øæº1­Øø╝superscript­Øæº2­Øæô­ØÉ¢g_{2}=\frac{1}{\pi M^{d}}\sum_{J\text{ even }}n_{J}^{(d)}\int_{0}^{1}\frac{dz}{z}z^{\frac{d}{2}}\rho_{J}(M^{2}/z)\left(1-\alpha z^{2}f(J)\right)\,. (63)

This is a good step to get an upper bound on g2subscript­Øæö2g_{2} since for ╬▒>0­Øø╝0\alpha>0 the integrand is mostly negative at large spin J­ØÉ¢J, except for a small region with z­Øæºz small. We use unitarity in two steps: first, we use positivity ¤üJÔëÑ0subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢0\rho_{J}\geq 0 to restrict the integral to the small-z­Øæºz region where the parenthesis is positive, where we can then use ¤üJÔëñ2subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢2\rho_{J}\leq 2. Thus:

g2Ôëñ2¤ÇÔÇïMdÔÇïÔêæJÔÇï┬áeven┬ánJ(d)ÔÇïÔê½0zmaxÔÇï(J)dÔÇïzzÔÇïzd2ÔÇï(1ÔêÆ╬▒ÔÇïz2ÔÇïfÔÇï(J))subscript­Øæö22­Ø£ïsuperscript­ØæÇ­Øææsubscript­ØÉ¢┬áeven┬ásuperscriptsubscript­Øæø­ØÉ¢­Øææsuperscriptsubscript0subscript­Øæºmax­ØÉ¢­Øææ­Øæº­Øæºsuperscript­Øæº­Øææ21­Øø╝superscript­Øæº2­Øæô­ØÉ¢g_{2}\leq\frac{2}{\pi M^{d}}\sum_{J\text{ even }}n_{J}^{(d)}\int_{0}^{z_{\rm max}(J)}\frac{dz}{z}z^{\frac{d}{2}}\left(1-\alpha z^{2}f(J)\right) (64)

where zmaxÔÇï(J)=minÔüí([╬▒ÔÇïfÔÇï(J)]ÔêÆ12,1)subscript­Øæºmax­ØÉ¢superscriptdelimited-[]­Øø╝­Øæô­ØÉ¢121z_{\rm max}(J)=\min([\alpha f(J)]^{-\frac{1}{2}},1) determines the region where the parenthesis is positive. The important point is that at large spin this region shrinks, which will ensure convergence of the J­ØÉ¢J sum. Effectively the region is bounded by impact parameter MÔÇïb~Ôê╝┬á<╬▒ÔêÆ1/4Ôê╝┬á<­ØæÇ~­ØæÅsuperscript­Øø╝14M\tilde{b}\mathrel{\hbox{\hbox to0.0pt{\lower 2.36806pt\hbox{$\sim$}\hss} \kern-3.00003pt\raise 1.72218pt\hbox{$<$}}}\alpha^{-1/4}. At small spins the full range is generally accessible. Letting zmaxÔÇï(J)=1subscript­Øæº­ØÉ¢1z_{\max}(J)=1 for JÔëñJÔêùÔÇï(╬▒)­ØÉ¢subscript­ØÉ¢­Øø╝J\leq J_{*}(\alpha), the sum splits as

g2Ôëñ2¤ÇÔÇïMdÔÇï[Ôêæ0ÔëñJÔëñJÔêùÔÇï(╬▒)nJ(d)ÔÇï(2dÔêÆ2ÔÇï╬▒ÔÇïfÔÇï(J)d+4)+8ÔÇï╬▒ÔêÆd4dÔÇï(d+4)ÔÇïÔêæJ>JÔêùÔÇï(╬▒)nJ(d)fÔÇï(J)d4].subscript­Øæö22­Ø£ïsuperscript­ØæÇ­Øæædelimited-[]subscript0­ØÉ¢subscript­ØÉ¢­Øø╝superscriptsubscript­Øæø­ØÉ¢­Øææ2­Øææ2­Øø╝­Øæô­ØÉ¢­Øææ48superscript­Øø╝­Øææ4­Øææ­Øææ4subscript­ØÉ¢subscript­ØÉ¢­Øø╝superscriptsubscript­Øæø­ØÉ¢­Øææ­Øæôsuperscript­ØÉ¢­Øææ4g_{2}\leq\frac{2}{\pi M^{d}}\left[\sum_{0\leq J\leq J_{*}(\alpha)}n_{J}^{(d)}\left(\frac{2}{d}-\frac{2\alpha f(J)}{d+4}\right)+\frac{8\alpha^{-\frac{d}{4}}}{d(d+4)}\sum_{J>J_{*}(\alpha)}\frac{n_{J}^{(d)}}{f(J)^{\frac{d}{4}}}\right]\,. (65)

Both sums run only over even spins. Since nJ(d)ÔêØJdÔêÆ3proportional-tosuperscriptsubscript­Øæø­ØÉ¢­Øææsuperscript­ØÉ¢­Øææ3n_{J}^{(d)}\propto J^{d-3} at large spin, the sum to infinity converges. This inequality is valid for any ╬▒>0­Øø╝0\alpha>0; with increasing ╬▒­Øø╝\alpha, the J=0,2­ØÉ¢02J=0,2 terms tend to increase whereas the rest decreases: the optimal bound with this method is obtained by minimizing over ╬▒­Øø╝\alpha. The dependence is rather non-linear (which is why we were not able to generalize the method to include more null constraints), but evaluating the sum numerically we find (in all dimensions we tried) that the optimum occurs with JÔêù=2subscript­ØÉ¢2J_{*}=2. In d=4­Øææ4d=4, for example, the optimal value ╬▒Ôêù=0.025subscript­Øø╝0.025\alpha_{*}=0.025, giving the analytic bound:

0Ôëñg2(4ÔÇï¤Ç)2Ôëñ0.794M4(d=4).formulae-sequence0subscript­Øæö2superscript4­Ø£ï20.794superscript­ØæÇ4­Øææ40\leq\frac{g_{2}}{(4\pi)^{2}}\leq\frac{0.794}{M^{4}}\qquad(d=4)\,. (66)

As expected, up to a standard loop factor, the coefficient of 12ÔÇï(Ôêé¤ò)412superscriptitalic-¤ò4\frac{1}{2}(\partial\phi)^{4} canÔÇÖt exceed order unity in units of the heavy scale. We stress that, contrary to other bounds in this paper, upper bounds on couplings at the cutoff scale cannot be straightforwardly interpreted in terms of Lagrangian parameters, since any EFT which saturates them is by definition strongly interacting already below the cutoff, making quantum corrections non-negligible. Rather, the bound may be interpreted as follows: among all observables which are linear in the S­ØæåS-matrix at the scale M­ØæÇM and which reduce to g2subscript­Øæö2g_{2} at weak coupling, there exists one which satisfies eq.┬á(66): namely, eq.┬á(62) with the quoted ╬▒Ôêùsubscript­Øø╝\alpha_{*}. This observable resides at the scale M­ØæÇM in the sense of eq.┬á(61).

Similar bounds in different spacetime dimensions are recorded in table 1; after normalizing with a suitable d­Øææd-dimension factor (closely related to n0(d)subscriptsuperscript­Øæø­Øææ0n^{(d)}_{0}) we find that the obtained bound is pretty stable in dimensions.

4 Numerically ruling-out: The allowed space of scalar EFTs

In this section, we summarize the obtained numerical results, focusing on d=3+1­Øææ31d=3+1 space-time dimensions. Treating the low-energy EFT to tree-level, we determine the space of EFT coefficients gnsubscript­Øæö­Øæøg_{n}, where the low-energy amplitude is parameterized as

Ôä│low=ÔêÆg2ÔÇï[1s+1t+1u]ÔêÆ╬╗+g2ÔÇï(s2+t2+u2)+g3ÔÇï(sÔÇïtÔÇïu)+g4ÔÇï(s2+t2+u2)2+g5ÔÇï(s2+t2+u2)ÔÇï(sÔÇïtÔÇïu)+Ôï»subscriptÔä│lowsuperscript­Øæö2delimited-[]1­Øæá1­Øæí1­Øæó­Ø£åsubscript­Øæö2superscript­Øæá2superscript­Øæí2superscript­Øæó2subscript­Øæö3­Øæá­Øæí­Øæósubscript­Øæö4superscriptsuperscript­Øæá2superscript­Øæí2superscript­Øæó22subscript­Øæö5superscript­Øæá2superscript­Øæí2superscript­Øæó2­Øæá­Øæí­ØæóÔï»\displaystyle\begin{split}\mathcal{M}_{\text{low}}=&-g^{2}\left[\frac{1}{s}+\frac{1}{t}+\frac{1}{u}\right]-\lambda\\ &+g_{2}(s^{2}+t^{2}+u^{2})+g_{3}(stu)+g_{4}(s^{2}+t^{2}+u^{2})^{2}+g_{5}(s^{2}+t^{2}+u^{2})(stu)+\cdots\end{split} (67)

Recall that it is convenient to introduce dimensionless EFT coefficients g~nsubscript~­Øæö­Øæø\tilde{g}_{n} normalized by g2subscript­Øæö2g_{2} and appropriate powers of the mass gap M­ØæÇM introduced in equation (26), since the numerical analysis is performed directly on these variables. We find optimal upper and lower bounds for g~3subscript~­Øæö3\tilde{g}_{3}, g~4subscript~­Øæö4\tilde{g}_{4}, and g~5subscript~­Øæö5\tilde{g}_{5}, given positivity of high-energy spectral densities ¤üJsubscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢\rho_{J}, using the optimization framework introduced above.

4.1 Bounds on individual coefficients

Let us begin by confining the value of individual EFT coefficients, being completely agnostic about all the others. Table 2 shows how these bounds depend on the number of crossing symmetry constraints kept. We conclude that, in d=3+1­Øææ31d=3+1 space-time, normalized EFT coefficients satisfy:

ÔêÆ10.346Ôëñg~3Ôëñ3,0Ôëñg~4Ôëñ12,ÔêÆ4.0960Ôëñg~5Ôëñ52.formulae-sequence10.346subscript~­Øæö330subscript~­Øæö4124.0960subscript~­Øæö552\displaystyle-10.346\leq\tilde{g}_{3}\leq 3,\qquad 0\leq\tilde{g}_{4}\leq\frac{1}{2},\qquad-4.0960\leq\tilde{g}_{5}\leq\frac{5}{2}\,. (68)

The simple, rational upper bounds are saturated by the spin-0 contribution to sum rules like eq.┬á(24).

While keeping constraints with higher Mandelstam degree n­Øæøn is feasible, runtime-wise, we found that the large-spin convergence was harder to control as we needed Jmax=­ØÆ¬ÔÇï(1000)subscript­ØÉ¢max­ØÆ¬1000J_{\text{max}}=\mathcal{O}(1000). However, for nÔëñ16­Øæø16n\leq 16, convergence is easily obtained for smaller spins. This is the reason why we stopped the table at n=16­Øæø16n=16; it would be interesting to understand how to stabilize the numerics at large n­Øæøn.

The g~3subscript~­Øæö3\tilde{g}_{3} lower bound is plotted as a function of 1/dimÔÇïN1dim­Øæü1/\text{dim}\,N in fig.┬á7, where dimÔÇïNdim­Øæü\text{dim}\,N is the dimension of the number of null constraints accessed. The dimension of this vector space is naturally monotonically increasing with the Mandelstam order n­Øæøn. Ideally, one would like to extrapolate the bounds to nÔåÆÔê×ÔåÆ­Øæøn\to\infty, however the approach is somewhat irregular and we didnÔÇÖt find a compelling fit function. Therefore, the recorded bounds are simply taken from our largest reliable value of n­Øæøn.

In d=6­Øææ6d=6 and d=10­Øææ10d=10, the analogous bounds take the values:

d=6::­Øææ6absent\displaystyle d=6: ÔêÆ8.13879Ôëñg~3Ôëñ3,0Ôëñg~4Ôëñ12,ÔêÆ3.03373Ôëñg~5Ôëñ52formulae-sequence8.13879subscript~­Øæö330subscript~­Øæö4123.03373subscript~­Øæö552\displaystyle\quad-8.13879\leq\tilde{g}_{3}\leq 3,\qquad 0\leq\tilde{g}_{4}\leq\tfrac{1}{2},\qquad-3.03373\leq\tilde{g}_{5}\leq\tfrac{5}{2} (69)
d=10::­Øææ10absent\displaystyle d=10: ÔêÆ7.04934Ôëñg~3Ôëñ3,0Ôëñg~4Ôëñ12,ÔêÆ2.51054Ôëñg~5Ôëñ52formulae-sequence7.04934subscript~­Øæö330subscript~­Øæö4122.51054subscript~­Øæö552\displaystyle\quad-7.04934\leq\tilde{g}_{3}\leq 3,\qquad 0\leq\tilde{g}_{4}\leq\tfrac{1}{2},\qquad-2.51054\leq\tilde{g}_{5}\leq\tfrac{5}{2} (70)

These upper and lower bounds were obtained by solving the optimization problems given in equations (43) and (48), respectively. For the n=16­Øæø16n=16 data points in the table, we kept spins up to 200 (in addition to J=Ôê×­ØÉ¢J=\infty). A full list of the bounds on the first 101010 coefficients g~nsubscript~­Øæö­Øæø\tilde{g}_{n} obtained with n=10­Øæø10n=10 (corresponding to 12 linearly independent null constraints) are presented in appendix B.

4.2 Two-dimensional allowed region in (g~3,g~4)subscript~­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö4(\tilde{g}_{3},\tilde{g}_{4}) plane

The ÔÇ£box boundsÔÇØ in eq.┬á(68) tell an incomplete story since they miss potential correlations between the coefficients. Exclusion plots on the (g~3,g~4)subscript~­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö4(\tilde{g}_{3},\tilde{g}_{4}) plane can be obtained following the constrained optimization framework presented at the end of section 3.2. Specifically, upper and lower bounding planes on g~4subscript~­Øæö4\tilde{g}_{4} were obtained by sampling g~3subscript~­Øæö3\tilde{g}_{3} at various points in the range allowed by eq.┬á(68). Repeating this process for a large number of sampling points generates a collection of linear inequalities, whose intersection defines a refined allowed region. Figure 8 depicts this region with over 100100100 sampling points along g~3subscript~­Øæö3\tilde{g}_{3}.

Further insight into the shape of the region can be obtained by noticing that the crossing symmetry constraints do not mix particles of spin 0 with the others, as noted in eq.┬á(60). This indicates that heavy spin-0 particles satisfy crossing on their own, as will be further discussed in the next section. For numerical purposes, this decoupling allows to refine the problem: any high-energy spectrum can be written as a positive sum of its spin-0 content, plus a unitary solution to crossing that only contains particles of spin JÔëÑ2­ØÉ¢2J\geq 2. The full allowed region is then simply the convex hull of the allowed regions for these two problems:

Entire region=Convex HullÔÇï[Spin-0+Spin-ÔÇïJÔëÑ2].Entire regionConvex Hulldelimited-[]Spin-0Spin-­ØÉ¢2\displaystyle\text{Entire region}=\text{Convex Hull}\left[\text{Spin-0}+\text{Spin-}J\geq 2\right]. (71)

As may be seen from the form of the g3subscript­Øæö3g_{3} sum rule (24), the two solutions are differentiated by the sign of g3subscript­Øæö3g_{3}: positive for Spin-0 and negative for Spin-JÔëÑ2­ØÉ¢2J\geq 2.

In our implementation of the dual problem, theories with only JÔëÑ2­ØÉ¢2J\geq 2 particles can be studied by simply dropping the positivity constraint for the functional action on J=0­ØÉ¢0J=0. The allowed regions for the Spin-0 and Spin-JÔëÑ2­ØÉ¢2J\geq 2 sub-problems are the narrow almond-shaped regions shown in fig.┬á9.

The shape of these regions is largely explained by a simple scaling argument: given any solution to crossing, scaling-up its overall mass scale will give a new solution. Starting from any allowed point (g~3,g~4)subscript~­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö4(\tilde{g}_{3},\tilde{g}_{4}), this generates an allowed path (╬▒ÔÇïg~3,╬▒2ÔÇïg~4)­Øø╝subscript~­Øæö3superscript­Øø╝2subscript~­Øæö4(\alpha\tilde{g}_{3},\alpha^{2}\tilde{g}_{4}) where 0Ôëñ╬▒Ôëñ10­Øø╝10\leq\alpha\leq 1. This explains the parabolic shape of the ÔÇ£underbelliesÔÇØ in fig.┬á9. In fact the Spin-0 almond is simply the convex hull of the parabola connecting (0,0)00(0,0) to (3,12)312(3,\frac{1}{2}). (This is qualitatively similar to what is found in the forward limit talks ; Bellazzini:2020cot .)

The Spin-JÔëÑ2­ØÉ¢2J\geq 2 region is more complicated ÔÇô while it also displays a parabolic underbelly near the origin, it fails to extend all the way to g~4=12subscript~­Øæö412\tilde{g}_{4}=\frac{1}{2}. The boundary must thus exhibit non-analytic behaviour at the end of the parabola, however we were unable to localize a kink that remains stable with varying the Mandelstam expansion order n­Øæøn, suggesting milder non-analyticity (such as a discontinuous second derivative). This qualitative feature is demonstrated by close-ups on the high-g~4subscript~­Øæö4\tilde{g}_{4} end of the allowed region are shown in fig.┬á10, which also indicates convergence with increasing n­Øæøn.

The line g~4=12subscript~­Øæö412\tilde{g}_{4}=\frac{1}{2} features two kinks: at (ÔêÆ10.19,0.5)10.190.5(-10.19,0.5) and (3,0.5)30.5(3,0.5), as shown in figure 10. Below we will find analytic expressions for the scattering amplitude at these kinks!

We chose the simplest and perhaps weakest method of calculating the exclusion region.
