Method of radials or normals have the potential more efficiently calculate these boundaries with higher fidelity near kinks Cordova:2019lot . For example, the normals maximization procedure explained extracts sharp features at regions of large curvature. Unfortunately, we were not able to use the normals method because it requires one to solve the ÔÇ£primalÔÇØ problem instead.

4.3 Three-dimensional allowed volume in (g~3,g~4,g~5)subscript~­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö4subscript~­Øæö5(\tilde{g}_{3},\tilde{g}_{4},\tilde{g}_{5})

Finally, we consider the space of EFT coefficients (g~3,g~4,g~5)subscript~­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö4subscript~­Øæö5(\tilde{g}_{3},\tilde{g}_{4},\tilde{g}_{5}) using a similar procedure to the 2ÔÇïd2­Øææ2d process. Once the 2ÔÇïd2­Øææ2d exclusion region is obtained, points in (g~3,g~4)subscript~­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö4(\tilde{g}_{3},\tilde{g}_{4}) are sampled from the exclusion region and then the optimal upper and lower bounds on g~5subscript~­Øæö5\tilde{g}_{5} with the associated hyperplanes are computed via the constrained optimization approach. Figure 11 shows that the 3ÔÇïd3­Øææ3d region is narrow, suggesting that S-matrix positivity is a potent constraint on the EFT.

Finally, the exclusion plot converges as the truncation order n­Øæøn is increased as shown in figures 10. In particular, the n=10­Øæø10n=10 and n=14­Øæø14n=14 regions overlap better than the n=4­Øæø4n=4 and n=10­Øæø10n=10 regions. Despite doubling the number of functions niÔÇï(m2,J)subscript­Øæø­Øæûsuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢n_{i}(m^{2},J) in the null constraints N­ØæüN to sharpen bounds between n=10­Øæø10n=10 and n=14­Øæø14n=14, the numerics show that they generate very similar regions. Therefore, it is sufficient to consider n=10­Øæø10n=10 in order to infer general features.

5 Analytically ruling-in: the two kinks with g~4=12subscript~­Øæö412\tilde{g}_{4}=\frac{1}{2}

It is interesting to ask whether the bounds on (g~3,g~4,ÔÇª)subscript~­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö4ÔÇª(\tilde{g}_{3},\tilde{g}_{4},\ldots) obtained in the preceding section are indeed optimal. We obtained these bounds using the so-called dual problem, which ÔÇ£rules-outÔÇØ more and more space as one adds null constraints. This contrasts with the primal problem, whereby the goal is to ÔÇ£rule-inÔÇØ coefficients of 2ÔåÆ2ÔåÆ222\to 2 S-matrices satisfying all the axioms. When results from both problems agree, the optimal solution is apprehended with complete confidence.

Systematic implementations of the primal problem have been proposed for generic field theories (without a large gap M­ØæÇM) Paulos:2017fhb ; Guerrieri:2018uew . Convergence of the dual and primal problems have also been studied for two-dimensional S-matrices Cordova:2019lot ; Guerrieri:2020kcs . We will not attempt to adapt these methods to our problem, but we will study simple special theories which can be ruled in analytically.

The (g~3,g~4)subscript~­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö4(\tilde{g}_{3},\tilde{g}_{4}) allowed region in fig.┬á8 prominently displays two kinks with g~4=12subscript~­Øæö412\tilde{g}_{4}=\tfrac{1}{2} (connected with a horizontal segment). This value is interesting, because the EFT parameter g2subscript­Øæö2g_{2} and g4subscript­Øæö4g_{4} satisfy the following sum rules:

g2=Ôƒ¿1m4Ôƒ®,g4=12ÔÇïÔƒ¿1m8Ôƒ®,formulae-sequencesubscript­Øæö2delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®1superscript­ØæÜ4subscript­Øæö412delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®1superscript­ØæÜ8g_{2}=\left\langle\frac{1}{m^{4}}\right\rangle\ ,\qquad g_{4}=\frac{1}{2}\left\langle\frac{1}{m^{8}}\right\rangle\,, (72)

where Ôƒ¿ÔïàÔƒ®delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®Ôïà\left\langle\,\cdot\,\right\rangle signifies a positive sum over states with mÔëÑM­ØæÜ­ØæÇm\geq M and different spin, as previously defined. The coefficient g4=g22ÔÇïM4subscript­Øæö4subscript­Øæö22superscript­ØæÇ4g_{4}=\frac{g_{2}}{2M^{4}} can only be realized if the high-energy theory contains only states at the single mass m=M­ØæÜ­ØæÇm=M!

The corresponding amplitudes are therefore rational functions with poles at s­Øæás, t­Øæít or u=m2­Øæósuperscript­ØæÜ2u=m^{2}. Furthermore, they must satisfy the boundedness property limsÔåÆ0Ôä│ÔÇï(s,t)s2=0subscriptÔåÆ­Øæá0Ôä│­Øæá­Øæísuperscript­Øæá20\lim_{s\to 0}\frac{\mathcal{M}(s,t)}{s^{2}}=0. It is easy to see that there are only two crossing-symmetric rational functions with these properties:

Ôä│spin-0=subscriptÔä│spin-0absent\displaystyle\mathcal{M}_{\text{spin-0}}= 1m2ÔêÆs+1m2ÔêÆt+1m2ÔêÆu,1superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæá1superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæí1superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæó\displaystyle\frac{1}{m^{2}-s}+\frac{1}{m^{2}-t}+\frac{1}{m^{2}-u}\,, (73)
Ôä│sÔÇïtÔÇïuÔÇï-pole=subscriptÔä│­Øæá­Øæí­Øæó-poleabsent\displaystyle\mathcal{M}_{stu\text{-pole}}= m4(m2ÔêÆs)ÔÇï(m2ÔêÆt)ÔÇï(m2ÔêÆu)ÔêÆ╬│ÔÇï(d)ÔÇïÔä│spin-0.superscript­ØæÜ4superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæásuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæó­Øø¥­ØææsubscriptÔä│spin-0\displaystyle\frac{m^{4}}{\left(m^{2}-s\right)\left(m^{2}-t\right)\left(m^{2}-u\right)}-\gamma(d)\mathcal{M}_{\text{spin-0}}\,.

Here, ╬│ÔÇï(d)­Øø¥­Øææ\gamma(d) is a coefficient that will ensure unitarity and positivity of the amplitude in d­Øææd-dimensional space-time. In particular, we have that

╬│ÔÇï(d)Ôëí492ÔÇïF1ÔÇï(12,1,dÔêÆ12,19).­Øø¥­Øææsubscript492subscript­ØÉ╣1121­Øææ1219\gamma(d)\equiv\frac{4}{9}\,_{2}F_{1}\left(\tfrac{1}{2},1,\tfrac{d-1}{2},\tfrac{1}{9}\right)\,. (74)

To show this, consider the spectral density of the first amplitude (its imaginary part) is supported entirely on spin-0. Following the decomposition used in fig.┬á9, we have then subtracted a constant multiple of Ôä│spinÔêÆ0subscriptÔä│spin0\mathcal{M}_{\text{spin}-0} to remove the spin-0 content from Ôä│sÔÇïtÔÇïuÔÇï-polesubscriptÔä│­Øæá­Øæí­Øæó-pole\mathcal{M}_{stu\text{-pole}}. To find the left kink, we should thus tune ╬│ÔÇï(d)­Øø¥­Øææ\gamma(d) to make the spectral density supported only on spins JÔëÑ2­ØÉ¢2J\geq 2.

In general, the Gegenbauer polynomials satisfy an orthogonality relation Correia:2020xtr that allows one to extract the coefficient:

fJÔÇï(s)=­ØÆ®d2ÔÇïÔê½ÔêÆ11­ØææzÔÇï(1ÔêÆz2)dÔêÆ42ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇï(z)ÔÇïÔä│ÔÇï(s,tÔÇï(z)),subscript­Øæô­ØÉ¢­Øæásubscript­ØÆ®­Øææ2superscriptsubscript11differential-d­Øæºsuperscript1superscript­Øæº2­Øææ42subscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢­ØæºÔä│­Øæá­Øæí­Øæº\displaystyle f_{J}(s)=\frac{\mathcal{N}_{d}}{2}\int_{-1}^{1}dz\left(1-z^{2}\right)^{\frac{d-4}{2}}\mathcal{P}_{J}(z)\mathcal{M}(s,t(z)), (75)

where z=1+2ÔÇïts­Øæº12­Øæí­Øæáz=1+\tfrac{2t}{s} and ­ØÆ®d=(16ÔÇï¤Ç)2ÔêÆd2╬ôÔÇï(dÔêÆ22)subscript­ØÆ®­Øææsuperscript16­Ø£ï2­Øææ2╬ô­Øææ22\mathcal{N}_{d}=\frac{(16\pi)^{\frac{2-d}{2}}}{\Gamma\left(\frac{d-2}{2}\right)} we find

¤üJÔÇï(s)|sÔÇïtÔÇïuÔÇï-pole=2ÔÇï¤ÇÔÇï­ØÆ®dÔÇïmdÔêÆ4ÔÇï╬┤ÔÇï(sÔêÆm2)ÔÇïÔê½ÔêÆ11­ØææzÔÇï(1ÔêÆz2)dÔêÆ42ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇï(z)ÔÇï(19ÔêÆz2ÔêÆ╬│ÔÇï(d)4).evaluated-atsubscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢­Øæá­Øæá­Øæí­Øæó-pole2­Ø£ïsubscript­ØÆ®­Øææsuperscript­ØæÜ­Øææ4­Øø┐­Øæásuperscript­ØæÜ2superscriptsubscript11differential-d­Øæºsuperscript1superscript­Øæº2­Øææ42subscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢­Øæº19superscript­Øæº2­Øø¥­Øææ4\rho_{J}(s)\big{|}_{stu\text{-pole}}=2\pi\mathcal{N}_{d}m^{d-4}\delta(s-m^{2})\int_{-1}^{1}dz\left(1-z^{2}\right)^{\frac{d-4}{2}}\mathcal{P}_{J}(z)\left(\frac{1}{9-z^{2}}-\frac{\gamma(d)}{4}\right)\,. (76)

Plugging in ­ØÆ½0ÔÇï(z)=1subscript­ØÆ½0­Øæº1\mathcal{P}_{0}(z)=1 and computing the integral, we find that the spin-0 component vanishes provided we choose:

╬│ÔÇï(d)=492ÔÇïF1ÔÇï(12,1,dÔêÆ12,19).­Øø¥­Øææsubscript492subscript­ØÉ╣1121­Øææ1219\gamma(d)=\frac{4}{9}\,_{2}F_{1}\left(\tfrac{1}{2},1,\tfrac{d-1}{2},\tfrac{1}{9}\right)\,. (77)

To verify that the partial waves are positive for JÔëÑ2­ØÉ¢2J\geq 2, one can use the Froissart-Gribov formula (see eq.┬á(2.53) of Correia:2020xtr ) to analytically integrate (76) in terms of a single residue at z=┬▒3­Øæºplus-or-minus3z=\pm 3; the integral is proportional to the function defined as QJ(d)ÔÇï(3)superscriptsubscript­Øæä­ØÉ¢­Øææ3Q_{J}^{(d)}(3) there, which is a positive hypergeometric function for any dÔëÑ3­Øææ3d\geq 3. We conclude that the amplitude Ôä│sÔÇïtÔÇïuÔÇï-polesubscriptÔä│­Øæá­Øæí­Øæó-pole\mathcal{M}_{stu\text{-pole}}, with the value (74), satisfies all the axioms of crossing symmetry, spin-2 Regge convergence, and positivity ¤üJÔÇï(s)ÔëÑ0subscript­Ø£î­ØÉ¢­Øæá0\rho_{J}(s)\geq 0! Of course, this does not imply that this amplitude can indeed be realized in some fully-fledged UV complete theory, only that it cannot be ruled out with our current methods.

Let us now situate the amplitudes Ôä│spin-0subscriptÔä│spin-0\mathcal{M}_{\text{spin-0}} and Ôä│sÔÇïtÔÇïuÔÇï-polesubscriptÔä│­Øæá­Øæí­Øæó-pole\mathcal{M}_{stu\text{-pole}} in the context of our numerical exclusion plots. By series expanding at small s,t,u­Øæá­Øæí­Øæós,t,u and comparing with the low-energy parameterization 8, it is straightforward to find that

Ôä│spin-0::subscriptÔä│spin-0absent\displaystyle\mathcal{M}_{\text{spin-0}}: (g3~,g~4,g~5)=(3m~2,12ÔÇïm~4,52ÔÇïm~6)~subscript­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö4subscript~­Øæö53superscript~­ØæÜ212superscript~­ØæÜ452superscript~­ØæÜ6\displaystyle\qquad(\tilde{g_{3}},\tilde{g}_{4},\tilde{g}_{5})=\left(\frac{3}{\tilde{m}^{2}},\frac{1}{2\tilde{m}^{4}},\frac{5}{2\tilde{m}^{6}}\right) (78)
Ôä│sÔÇïtÔÇïuÔÇï-pole::subscriptÔä│­Øæá­Øæí­Øæó-poleabsent\displaystyle\mathcal{M}_{stu\text{-pole}}: (g3~,g~4,g~5)=(2m~2ÔÇï3ÔÇï╬│ÔÇï(d)ÔêÆ12ÔÇï╬│ÔÇï(d)ÔêÆ1,12ÔÇïm~4,1m~6ÔÇï5ÔÇï╬│ÔÇï(d)ÔêÆ22ÔÇï╬│ÔÇï(d)ÔêÆ1),~subscript­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö4subscript~­Øæö52superscript~­ØæÜ23­Øø¥­Øææ12­Øø¥­Øææ112superscript~­ØæÜ41superscript~­ØæÜ65­Øø¥­Øææ22­Øø¥­Øææ1\displaystyle\qquad(\tilde{g_{3}},\tilde{g}_{4},\tilde{g}_{5})=\left(\frac{2}{\tilde{m}^{2}}\frac{3\gamma(d)-1}{2\gamma(d)-1},\frac{1}{2\tilde{m}^{4}},\frac{1}{\tilde{m}^{6}}\frac{5\gamma(d)-2}{2\gamma(d)-1}\right), (79)

where m~ÔëímM~­ØæÜ­ØæÜ­ØæÇ\tilde{m}\equiv\frac{m}{M}. At m~=1~­ØæÜ1\tilde{m}=1 and d=4­Øææ4d=4, the spin-00 model produces the upper-right kink at (3,12)312(3,\tfrac{1}{2}), whereas the sÔÇïtÔÇïu­Øæá­Øæí­Øæóstu-pole model gives (g3~,g~4)=(6ÔÇï2ÔÇïlogÔüí2ÔêÆ14ÔÇïlogÔüí2ÔêÆ3,12)Ôëê(ÔêÆ10.19196,0.5)~subscript­Øæö3subscript~­Øæö462214231210.191960.5(\tilde{g_{3}},\tilde{g}_{4})=(6\frac{2\log 2-1}{4\log 2-3},\tfrac{1}{2})\approx(-10.19196,0.5) which precisely matches with the numerical value in fig.┬á10! We also found agreement with the numerical position of the kink in d=6­Øææ6d=6 and d=10­Øææ10d=10. As dÔåÆÔê×ÔåÆ­Øææd\to\infty, the kink converges to (ÔêÆ6,ÔêÆ12)612(-6,-\frac{1}{2}).

The fact that the amplitude Ôä│sÔÇïtÔÇïuÔÇï-polesubscriptÔä│­Øæá­Øæí­Øæó-pole\mathcal{M}_{stu\text{-pole}} realizes a negative coefficient for sÔÇïtÔÇïu­Øæá­Øæí­Øæóstu (i.e. g3<0)g_{3}<0) suggests that it would be impossible to prove irreversibility of six-dimensional renormalization group flow using only crossing and positivity of 2ÔåÆ2ÔåÆ222\to 2 scattering Elvang:2012st . This contrasts the successful four-dimensional case, in which the a­ØæÄa-theorem was related to positivity of g2subscript­Øæö2g_{2} Komargodski:2011vj .

The convex hull of the space of the two amplitudes Ôä│spin-0subscriptÔä│spin-0\mathcal{M}_{\text{spin-0}} and Ôä│sÔÇïtÔÇïuÔÇï-polesubscriptÔä│­Øæá­Øæí­Øæó-pole\mathcal{M}_{stu\text{-pole}}, for different values of the mass m­ØæÜm, generates the region with parabolic boundaries shown in fig.┬á12. The parabolic ÔÇ£underbelliesÔÇØ are simply the dimensionally-rescaled theories at the kinks. This region is analytically ÔÇ£ruled inÔÇØ.

One notices that a sliver obtained via numerics is not in the span of these simple analytic models. Since the upper-left arc of this sliver cannot be generated by the convex hull of (possibly rescaled) discrete theories, we attribute it to a continuous one-parameter family of ÔÇ£extremal theoriesÔÇØ which terminate at the kink. It would be interesting to find an analytic expressions for this family.

One may wonder if this family of extremal theories terminates at a second kink; we did not locate any stable candidate in the numerics. This suggests that the family instead disappears inside its own convex hull. Naturally this would occur at the point where its slope becomes tangent to the parabola of dimensionally-rescaled theories originating from that point, dÔÇïg3g4=g32ÔÇïg4­Øææsubscript­Øæö3subscript­Øæö4subscript­Øæö32subscript­Øæö4\frac{dg_{3}}{g_{4}}=\frac{g_{3}}{2g_{4}}. In this scenario, only the second derivative of the boundary shape would be discontinuous, explaining the difficulty in locating it numerically.

We find it remarkable that two simple analytic models almost span the entire region obtained via numerics: to a good approximation (up to the missing sliver), a scalar EFT is compatible with causality and unitarity if it is a positive linear combination of the models in eq.┬á(73).

6 Concluding remarks

In this work we showed, in the case of a scalar field theory, that the space of low effective field theories is sharply constrained by positivity of the S-matrix. Dimensional analysis teaches us to expect a low-energy coupling of mass dimension n­Øæøn to be suppressed by a factor of 1/Mn1superscript­ØæÇ­Øæø1/M^{n}, where M­ØæÇM is the mass of new heavy states. Our main finding is simple: in any causal and unitary theory, dimensional analysis scaling is a theorem.

More precisely, for a theory of a single identical real scalar, we showed that dimensionless ratios of the form gk/g2ÔÇïM#subscript­Øæö­Øæÿsubscript­Øæö2superscript­ØæÇ#g_{k}/g_{2}M^{\#}, where g2subscript­Øæö2g_{2} is the coefficient of 12ÔÇï(Ôêé¤ò)412superscriptitalic-¤ò4\tfrac{1}{2}(\partial\phi)^{4}, are bounded above and below by finite constants of order unity. Our method does not assume that physics above the scale M­ØæÇM is weakly coupled, only that it is consistent with unitarity and causality. The technical assumption is the convergence of double-subtracted dispersion relations in 2ÔåÆ2ÔåÆ222\to 2 scattering. We then separately bounded g2subscript­Øæö2g_{2} itself in eq.┬á(66).

To our (possibly incomplete) knowledge, this is the first time that two-sided bounds are obtained for interactions that vanish in the forward limit, such as the six-derivative ÔÇ£sÔÇïtÔÇïu­Øæá­Øæí­ØæóstuÔÇØ contact interaction g3subscript­Øæö3g_{3} (see eqs.┬á(5) and (68)). The key ingredient was to use ÔÇ£null constraintsÔÇØ (for example eq.┬á(25)): integrals over the high-energy spectral density which must vanish by crossing symmetry, and which limit the contribution of higher-spin particles. A systematic procedure to extract optimal bounds was presented. The precise form of the null constraints are affected by low-energy self-interactions (i.e. loops within the low-energy EFT), whose effects would be interesting to investigate. It would be interesting to assess if bounds of this type are closely saturated in specific low-energy processes, for example pion scattering. In this case, a generalization to non-identical scalars might be necessary.

In this paper we ignored gravity. A graviton pole would cause the B2ÔÇï(t)subscript­ØÉÁ2­ØæíB_{2}(t) sum rule to diverge in the forward limit, invalidating conclusions from a Taylor series around t=0­Øæí0t=0. However the X2ÔÇï(t)subscript­Øæï2­ØæíX_{2}(t) null constraints in eq.┬á(82) should remain valid for t<0­Øæí0t<0 and their implications are worth investigating. Intuitively, one may anticipate that the graviton pole will somewhat weaken the bounds on scalar scattering Adams:2006sv ; Tokuda:2020mlf ; Alberte:2020jsk (see also Bellazzini:2019xts ). However, following the general principle that self-consistent spinning S-matrices are harder to come by, interactions involving gravitons are likely to be sharply constrained by similar methods, possibly extending Camanho:2014apa ; Hamada:2018dde ; deRham:2018qqo or addressing conjectures of Chowdhury:2019kaq .

For non-gravitational scalar scattering, we found that crossing symmetry does not mix heavy spinning and spinless states. The allowed 2ÔåÆ2ÔåÆ222\to 2 low-energy S-matrices are positive sums of those two sectors. These are respectively (almost completely) spanned by the two simple analytic models in eq.┬á(73), which realize theories at kinks. Perhaps this is a tantalizing hint that the collection of valid EFTs is not so vast after all.

Acknowledgements.
We thank Dalimil Maz├á─ì, Leonardo Rastelli, David Simmons-Duffin and Anh-Khoi Trinh for useful conversations and Cl├®ment Virally for technical assistance. Work of VVD is supported by the National Science and Engineering Council of Canada. Work of SCH is supported by the National Science and Engineering Council of Canada, the Canada Research Chair program, the Fonds de Recherche du Qu├®bec - Nature et Technologies, and the Simons Collaboration on the Nonperturbative Bootstrap.

Appendix A A basis of crossing symmetry constraints

In this section we present a complete basis of vanishing heavy averages, which vanish in any theory with no branch cut below M2superscript­ØæÇ2M^{2} (i.e. when neglecting low-energy loops). They are organized as functions XkÔÇï(t)subscript­Øæï­Øæÿ­ØæíX_{k}(t) where k­Øæÿk is an even integer representing the number of subtractions.

We first give a pedestrian argument considering the unsubtracted case, X0subscript­Øæï0X_{0}, and then make a general argument exploiting crossing symmetry.

As a warm-up, let us first consider a ÔÇ£superboundedÔÇØ theory where limsÔåÆÔê×Ôä│ÔÇï(s,t)ÔåÆ0ÔåÆsubscriptÔåÆ­ØæáÔä│­Øæá­Øæí0\lim_{s\to\infty}\mathcal{M}(s,t)\to 0 for t<0­Øæí0t<0, so that unsubtracted dispersion relations converge, and where the low-energy amplitude is polynomial, corresponding to g=0­Øæö0g=0 in (5). In the superbounded case the first sum rule is B0subscript­ØÉÁ0B_{0} from eq.┬á(20):

B0:g0+2ÔÇïg2ÔÇït2+2ÔÇïg4ÔÇït4+ÔÇª=Ôƒ¿2ÔÇïm2+tm2+tÔÇï­ØÆ½ÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïtm2)Ôƒ®:subscript­ØÉÁ0subscript­Øæö02subscript­Øæö2superscript­Øæí22subscript­Øæö4superscript­Øæí4ÔÇªdelimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®2superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæí­ØÆ½12­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2B_{0}:\ g_{0}+2g_{2}t^{2}+2g_{4}t^{4}+\ldots=\left\langle\frac{2m^{2}+t}{m^{2}+t}\mathcal{P}\left(1+\frac{2t}{m^{2}}\right)\right\rangle (80)

where we relabelled the constant term in the amplitude as g0=ÔêÆ╬╗subscript­Øæö0­Ø£åg_{0}=-\lambda. Viewed as a function of t­Øæít this sum rule is marginally useful because it involves infinitely many unknowns on the left-hand-side and one loses control when ÔêÆt­Øæí-t becomes of order M2superscript­ØæÇ2M^{2}. However, each gksubscript­Øæö­Øæÿg_{k} with kÔëÑ2­Øæÿ2k\geq 2 can be computed by some other sum rule: for example, the tÔåÆ0ÔåÆ­Øæí0t\to 0 limit of B2subscript­ØÉÁ2B_{2} measures g2subscript­Øæö2g_{2}, B4ÔÇï(0)subscript­ØÉÁ40B_{4}(0) measures g4subscript­Øæö4g_{4}, etc. Dividing by an overall t­Øæít for future convenience, we can package these into an infinite set of null constraints:

00\displaystyle 0 =Ôƒ¿2ÔÇïm2+ttÔÇï(m2+t)ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïtm2)Ôƒ®ÔêÆÔêæk=0Ôê×BkhighÔÇï(0)ÔÇït2ÔÇïkÔêÆ1absentdelimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®2superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæí­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢12­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2superscriptsubscript­Øæÿ0superscriptsubscript­ØÉÁ­Øæÿhigh0superscript­Øæí2­Øæÿ1\displaystyle=\left\langle\frac{2m^{2}+t}{t(m^{2}+t)}\mathcal{P}_{J}\left(1+\frac{2t}{m^{2}}\right)\right\rangle-\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}B_{k}^{\rm high}(0)t^{2k-1} (81)
=Ôƒ¿X0ÔÇï(t;m2,j)Ôƒ®whereX0ÔÇï(t;m2,J)Ôëí(2ÔÇïm2+t)ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïtm2)tÔÇï(m2+t)ÔêÆ2ÔÇïm4ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1)tÔÇï(m4ÔêÆt2).formulae-sequenceabsentdelimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®subscript­Øæï0­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæùwheresubscript­Øæï0­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢2superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢12­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæí2superscript­ØæÜ4subscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢1­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ4superscript­Øæí2\displaystyle=\left\langle X_{0}(t;m^{2},j)\right\rangle\quad\mbox{where}\quad X_{0}(t;m^{2},J)\equiv\frac{(2m^{2}+t)\mathcal{P}_{J}\left(1+\frac{2t}{m^{2}}\right)}{t(m^{2}+t)}-\frac{2m^{4}\mathcal{P}_{J}(1)}{t(m^{4}-t^{2})}\,.

X0ÔÇï(t)subscript­Øæï0­ØæíX_{0}(t) defines a sensible sum rule for any 0<ÔêÆt<M20­Øæísuperscript­ØæÇ20<-t<M^{2}, and Taylor-expanding around t=0­Øæí0t=0 gives an infinite number of averages which vanish by crossing symmetry (if unsubtracted sum rules converge).

Repeating the same manipulations for subtracted sum rules, we find that to cancel the cancel the generic power of t­Øæít in B2ÔÇï(t)subscript­ØÉÁ2­ØæíB_{2}(t) one must also both the BkÔÇï(0)subscript­ØÉÁ­Øæÿ0B_{k}(0) and its first derivative around t=0­Øæí0t=0; there is then a unique solution: Ôƒ¿X2ÔÇï(t;m2,J)Ôƒ®=0delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®subscript­Øæï2­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢0\langle X_{2}(t;m^{2},J)\rangle=0 where

X2ÔÇï(t;m2,J)=(2ÔÇïm2+t)ÔÇï(­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïtm2)ÔêÆ­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1))m2ÔÇït2ÔÇï(m2+t)2ÔêÆ4ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇ▓ÔÇï(1)tÔÇïm2ÔÇï(m4ÔêÆt2).subscript­Øæï2­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢2superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢12­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2subscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢1superscript­ØæÜ2superscript­Øæí2superscriptsuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæí24superscriptsubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢ÔÇ▓1­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2superscript­ØæÜ4superscript­Øæí2X_{2}(t;m^{2},J)=\frac{(2m^{2}+t)\left(\mathcal{P}_{J}\left(1+\frac{2t}{m^{2}}\right)-\mathcal{P}_{J}(1)\right)}{m^{2}t^{2}(m^{2}+t)^{2}}-\frac{4\mathcal{P}_{J}^{\prime}(1)}{tm^{2}(m^{4}-t^{2})}\,. (82)

For any 0<ÔêÆt<M20­Øæísuperscript­ØæÇ20<-t<M^{2}, this enjoys the high-energy convergence as a double-subtracted dispersion relation, whence the subscript. Setting t=0­Øæí0t=0 this reproduces the n4subscript­Øæø4n_{4} sum rule in eq.┬á(60). Finally, we record explicitly the four-subtracted version obtained with the same method:

X4ÔÇï(t;m2,J)=(2ÔÇïm2+t)ÔÇï(­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïtm2)ÔêÆ­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1))m4ÔÇït3ÔÇï(m2+t)3ÔêÆ2ÔÇï(2ÔÇïm4ÔêÆ3ÔÇïtÔÇï(m2+t))ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇ▓ÔÇï(1)m8ÔÇït2ÔÇï(m2ÔêÆt)ÔÇï(m2+t)2ÔêÆ4ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇ▓ÔÇ▓ÔÇï(1)m8ÔÇïtÔÇï(m4ÔêÆt2).subscript­Øæï4­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢2superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢12­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2subscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢1superscript­ØæÜ4superscript­Øæí3superscriptsuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæí322superscript­ØæÜ43­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísuperscriptsubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢ÔÇ▓1superscript­ØæÜ8superscript­Øæí2superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísuperscriptsuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæí24superscriptsubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢ÔÇ▓ÔÇ▓1superscript­ØæÜ8­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ4superscript­Øæí2X_{4}(t;m^{2},J)=\frac{(2m^{2}+t)\left(\mathcal{P}_{J}\left(1+\frac{2t}{m^{2}}\right)-\mathcal{P}_{J}(1)\right)}{m^{4}t^{3}(m^{2}+t)^{3}}-\frac{2(2m^{4}-3t(m^{2}+t))\mathcal{P}_{J}^{\prime}(1)}{m^{8}t^{2}(m^{2}-t)(m^{2}+t)^{2}}-\frac{4\mathcal{P}_{J}^{\prime\prime}(1)}{m^{8}t(m^{4}-t^{2})}\,. (83)

A.1 Derivation using dispersion relations in three channels

Let us now present the general case along with a direct derivation. The idea is to combine dispersion relations in all three channels. Consider the following identity:

XkÔÇï(tÔÇ▓): 0=[Ôê«Cs+Ôê«Ct+Ôê«Cu]ÔÇïdÔÇïsÔêºdÔÇït(2ÔÇï¤ÇÔÇïi)2ÔÇïsÔÇïtÔÇï(tÔêÆtÔÇ▓)ÔÇïÔä│ÔÇï(s,t)[sÔÇïtÔÇï(s+t)]k/2,:subscript­Øæï­Øæÿsuperscript­ØæíÔÇ▓ 0delimited-[]subscriptcontour-integralsubscript­ØÉÂ­Øæásubscriptcontour-integralsubscript­ØÉÂ­Øæísubscriptcontour-integralsubscript­ØÉÂ­Øæó­Øææ­Øæá­Øææ­Øæísuperscript2­Ø£ï­Øæû2­Øæá­Øæí­Øæísuperscript­ØæíÔÇ▓Ôä│­Øæá­Øæísuperscriptdelimited-[]­Øæá­Øæí­Øæá­Øæí­Øæÿ2X_{k}(t^{\prime}):\ 0=\left[\oint_{C_{s}}+\oint_{C_{t}}+\oint_{C_{u}}\right]\frac{ds\wedge dt}{(2\pi i)^{2}st(t-t^{\prime})}\frac{\mathcal{M}(s,t)}{[st(s+t)]^{k/2}}\,, (84)

where each contour is a product of circles: Cs=CirclesÔê╝0ÔêºCircletÔåÆÔê×subscript­ØÉÂ­ØæásubscriptCirclesimilar-to­Øæá0subscriptCircleÔåÆ­ØæíC_{s}={\rm Circle}_{s\sim 0}\wedge{\rm Circle}_{t\to\infty}, Ct=CircletÔê╝0,tÔÇ▓ÔêºCircleuÔåÆÔê×subscript­ØÉÂ­ØæísubscriptCirclesimilar-to­Øæí0superscript­ØæíÔÇ▓subscriptCircleÔåÆ­ØæóC_{t}={\rm Circle}_{t\sim 0,t^{\prime}}\wedge{\rm Circle}_{u\to\infty} and Cu=CircleuÔê╝0ÔêºCirclesÔåÆÔê×subscript­ØÉÂ­ØæósubscriptCirclesimilar-to­Øæó0subscriptCircleÔåÆ­ØæáC_{u}={\rm Circle}_{u\sim 0}\wedge{\rm Circle}_{s\to\infty}. The first contour implements a fixed-s­Øæás sum rule, which we then evaluate at sÔåÆ0ÔåÆ­Øæá0s\to 0, and similar for the others, but note that the fixed-t­Øæít relation is evaluated at both t=0­Øæí0t=0 and t=tÔÇ▓­Øæísuperscript­ØæíÔÇ▓t=t^{\prime}. All the integrals vanish (for kÔëÑ2­Øæÿ2k\geq 2) due to the vanishing at large-t­Øæít of Ôä│ÔÇï(s,t)/t2Ôä│­Øæá­Øæísuperscript­Øæí2\mathcal{M}(s,t)/t^{2}. (The contours around 00 are a bit dangerous, since the integrals around say large-t­Øæít converges only when s<0­Øæá0s<0; this should not be a problem since (ignoring EFT loops) one can interpret the residue as extracting the coefficient of 1/s1­Øæá1/s in the Laurent expansion as sÔåÆ0ÔêÆÔåÆ­Øæásuperscript0s\to 0^{-}.) The trick is then to deform the arcs at infinity to pick high-energy cuts and low-energy poles, as in fig┬á3 of the main text.

What is nice with the above contour is that all double-residues cancel because of antisymmetry of the contours. For the double residue at (s,t)=(0,tÔÇ▓)­Øæá­Øæí0superscript­ØæíÔÇ▓(s,t)=(0,t^{\prime}) the cancellation simply follows from mismatching orientations, since the contours Cssubscript­ØÉÂ­ØæáC_{s} and Ctsubscript­ØÉÂ­ØæíC_{t} compute the same double-residue in opposite orders:

0=CirclesÔê╝0ÔêºCircletÔê╝tÔÇ▓+CircletÔê╝tÔÇ▓ÔêºCirclesÔê╝0.0subscriptCirclesimilar-to­Øæá0subscriptCirclesimilar-to­Øæísuperscript­ØæíÔÇ▓subscriptCirclesimilar-to­Øæísuperscript­ØæíÔÇ▓subscriptCirclesimilar-to­Øæá00={\rm Circle}_{s\sim 0}\wedge{\rm Circle}_{t\sim t^{\prime}}+{\rm Circle}_{t\sim t^{\prime}}\wedge{\rm Circle}_{s\sim 0}\,. (85)

Alternatively, in practice, one may think of the integral 12ÔÇï¤ÇÔÇïiÔÇïÔê½CirclesÔê╝012­Ø£ï­ØæûsubscriptsubscriptCirclesimilar-to­Øæá0\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{{\rm Circle}_{s\sim 0}} as just a residue, defined by plucking dÔÇïssÔêºlimit-from­Øææ­Øæá­Øæá\frac{ds}{s}\wedge from the left of the differential form it multiplies, and the integral vanishes because the thus-defined residue operation is antisymmetric. The nested poles at (s,t)=(0,0)­Øæá­Øæí00(s,t)=(0,0) require more care (one has to perform a blow-up) since all three contours contribute, but one still finds a perfect cancellation, as one may verify explicitly:

(Rest=0Ress=0+Ress=0Rest=0+Rest=0Ress=ÔêÆt)ÔÇïPolyÔÇï(s,t)(sÔÇïtÔÇï(s+t))nÔÇïdÔÇïsÔêºdÔÇït=0subscriptRes­Øæí0subscriptRes­Øæá0subscriptRes­Øæá0subscriptRes­Øæí0subscriptRes­Øæí0subscriptRes­Øæá­ØæíPoly­Øæá­Øæísuperscript­Øæá­Øæí­Øæá­Øæí­Øæø­Øææ­Øæá­Øææ­Øæí0\left(\ \operatorname*{Res}\limits_{t=0}\operatorname*{Res}\limits_{s=0}\ +\ \operatorname*{Res}\limits_{s=0}\operatorname*{Res}\limits_{t=0}\ +\ \operatorname*{Res}\limits_{t=0}\operatorname*{Res}\limits_{s=-t}\right)\frac{{\rm Poly}(s,t)}{(st(s+t))^{n}}ds\wedge dt=0 (86)

for any polynomial numerator. This is curiously reminiscent of the Jacobi identity.

The upshot is that the integral (84) is orthogonal to any tree-level low-energy EFT amplitude (whether or not it is s­Øæás-t­Øæít-u­Øæóu symmetric), it gives purely a constraint on high-energy cuts. Similar constraints would follow for any choice of (rational) denominator. Each term in eq.┬á(84) contributes cuts in two channels, for example the Cssubscript­ØÉÂ­ØæáC_{s} term contributes

Ôƒ¿Ress=0(1m2ÔÇï(tÔÇ▓ÔêÆm2)+1(m2+s)ÔÇï(m2+s+tÔÇ▓))Ôüím2ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïsm2)sÔÇï(sÔÇïm2ÔÇï(m2+s))k/2Ôƒ®delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®subscriptRes­Øæá01superscript­ØæÜ2superscript­ØæíÔÇ▓superscript­ØæÜ21superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæásuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæásuperscript­ØæíÔÇ▓superscript­ØæÜ2subscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢12­Øæásuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæásuperscript­Øæásuperscript­ØæÜ2superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæá­Øæÿ2\left\langle\operatorname*{Res}\limits_{s=0}\left(\frac{1}{m^{2}(t^{\prime}-m^{2})}+\frac{1}{(m^{2}+s)(m^{2}+s+t^{\prime})}\right)\frac{m^{2}\mathcal{P}_{J}\left(1+\frac{2s}{m^{2}}\right)}{s(sm^{2}(m^{2}+s))^{k/2}}\right\rangle (87)

where the two terms in the parenthesis come from the t­Øæít and u­Øæóu-channel heavy cuts, respectively. If we did not assume sÔêÆtÔêÆu­Øæá­Øæí­Øæós{-}t{-}u symmetry, we would get a relation between the three spectral densities. Here we record only the simplified result assuming that all the spectral densities are the same: 0=Ôƒ¿XkÔÇï(t;m2,J)Ôƒ®0delimited-Ôƒ¿Ôƒ®subscript­Øæï­Øæÿ­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢0=\langle X_{k}(t;m^{2},J)\rangle where

XkÔÇï(t;m2,J)=subscript­Øæï­Øæÿ­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­ØÉ¢absent\displaystyle X_{k}(t;m^{2},J)= 2ÔÇïm2+ttÔÇï(m2+t)ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïtm2)(tÔÇïm2ÔÇï(m2+t))k/22superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæí­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢12­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2superscript­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæí­Øæÿ2\displaystyle\frac{2m^{2}+t}{t(m^{2}+t)}\frac{\mathcal{P}_{J}\left(1+\frac{2t}{m^{2}}\right)}{(tm^{2}(m^{2}+t))^{k/2}} (88)
ÔêÆRess=0[(2ÔÇïm2+s)ÔÇï(m2ÔêÆs)ÔÇï(m2+2ÔÇïs)sÔÇï(tÔêÆs)ÔÇï(m2+s)ÔÇï(m2ÔêÆt)ÔÇï(m2+s+t)ÔÇï­ØÆ½JÔÇï(1+2ÔÇïsm2)(sÔÇïm2ÔÇï(m2+s))k/2].subscriptRes­Øæá02superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæásuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæásuperscript­ØæÜ22­Øæá­Øæá­Øæí­Øæásuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæásuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæísuperscript­ØæÜ2­Øæá­Øæísubscript­ØÆ½­ØÉ¢12­Øæásuperscript­ØæÜ2superscript­Øæásuperscript­ØæÜ2superscript­ØæÜ2­Øæá­Øæÿ2\displaystyle\ -\operatorname*{Res}\limits_{s=0}\left[\frac{(2m^{2}+s)(m^{2}-s)(m^{2}+2s)}{s(t-s)(m^{2}+s)(m^{2}-t)(m^{2}+s+t)}\frac{\mathcal{P}_{J}\left(1+\frac{2s}{m^{2}}\right)}{(sm^{2}(m^{2}+s))^{k/2}}\right]\,.

This is the main result of this appendix. For any even kÔëÑ2­Øæÿ2k\geq 2 and ÔêÆM2<t<0superscript­ØæÇ2­Øæí0-M^{2}<t<0 we expect these to be physical sum rules (i.e. convergent in theories where double-subtracted dispersion relations converge) orthogonal to any tree-level low-energy amplitude. For k=0,2,4­Øæÿ024k=0,2,4 this reproduces the formulas quoted in eqs.┬á(81)-(83).

Expanding at small t­Øæít, the XkÔÇï(t)subscript­Øæï­Øæÿ­ØæíX_{k}(t) sum rules admit regular Taylor series, which reproduce precisely the sum rules recorded in eq.┬á(60). Namely, the coefficient of tnsuperscript­Øæí­Øæøt^{n} in Xksubscript­Øæï­ØæÿX_{k} has degree 3ÔÇïk2+n+13­Øæÿ2­Øæø1\frac{3k}{2}+n+1 in 1/m21superscript­ØæÜ21/m^{2}, so the first case is X2ÔÇï(0)Ôê╝g4similar-tosubscript­Øæï20subscript­Øæö4X_{2}(0)\sim g_{4}. The first time one gets two X­ØæïX sum rules is at weight 7, where X2ÔÇ▓ÔÇ▓ÔÇ▓ÔÇï(0)superscriptsubscript­Øæï2ÔÇ▓ÔÇ▓ÔÇ▓0X_{2}^{\prime\prime\prime}(0) and X4ÔÇï(0)subscript­Øæï40X_{4}(0) span g7subscript­Øæö7g_{7} and g7ÔÇ▓superscriptsubscript­Øæö7ÔÇ▓g_{7}^{\prime}. The number of X­ØæïX sum rules per degree increases every 3 degree because of the 1/(sÔÇïtÔÇïu)k/21superscript­Øæá­Øæí­Øæó­Øæÿ21/(stu)^{k/2} factor in eq.┬á(84). The number of X­ØæïX sum rules thus agrees precisely with the counting below eq.┬á(60). We conclude that the XkÔÇï(t)subscript­Øæï­Øæÿ­ØæíX_{k}(t) sum rules are a complete basis of sum rules orthogonal to tree-level EFTs! As mentioned below eq.┬á(61), when EFT loops are included these sum rules may average to nonzero but computable quantities.

Appendix B Bounds on operators up to order s10superscript­Øæá10s^{10}

In table 3 we record numerical bounds on various EFT coefficients in four spacetime dimensions.

References

  • (1) A.┬áAdams, N.┬áArkani-Hamed, S.┬áDubovsky, A.┬áNicolis and R.┬áRattazzi, Causality, analyticity and an IR obstruction to UV completion, JHEP 10 (2006) 014 [hep-th/0602178].
  • (2) A.┬áMartin, Scattering Theory: Unitarity, Analyticity and Crossing, vol.┬á3 (1969), 10.1007/BFb0101043.
  • (3) S.M.┬áRoy, Exact integral equation for pion pion scattering involving only physical region partial waves, Phys. Lett. B 36 (1971) 353.
  • (4) G.┬áColangelo, J.┬áGasser and H.┬áLeutwyler, ¤ÇÔÇï¤Ç­Ø£ï­Ø£ï\pi\pi scattering, Nucl. Phys. B 603 (2001) 125 [hep-ph/0103088].
  • (5) I.┬áCaprini, G.┬áColangelo, J.┬áGasser and H.┬áLeutwyler, On the precision of the theoretical predictions for pi pi scattering, Phys. Rev. D 68 (2003) 074006 [hep-ph/0306122].
  • (6) T.N.┬áPham and T.N.┬áTruong, Evaluation of the Derivative Quartic Terms of the Meson Chiral Lagrangian From Forward Dispersion Relation, Phys. Rev. D 31 (1985) 3027.
  • (7) B.┬áAnanthanarayan, D.┬áToublan and G.┬áWanders, Consistency of the chiral pion pion scattering amplitudes with axiomatic constraints, Phys. Rev. D 51 (1995) 1093 [hep-ph/9410302].
  • (8) X.O.┬áCamanho, J.D.┬áEdelstein, J.┬áMaldacena and A.┬áZhiboedov, Causality Constraints on Corrections to the Graviton Three-Point Coupling, JHEP 02 (2016) 020 [1407.5597].
  • (9) N.┬áAfkhami-Jeddi, T.┬áHartman, S.┬áKundu and A.┬áTajdini, Einstein gravity 3-point functions from conformal field theory, JHEP 12 (2017) 049 [1610.09378].
  • (10) C.┬áCheung and G.N.┬áRemmen, Positive Signs in Massive Gravity, JHEP 04 (2016) 002 [1601.04068].
  • (11) N.┬áArkani-Hamed and Y.-T.┬áHuang, Positive geometry of effective field theory, lectures at the CERN winter school on supergravity, strings and gauge theory (2019); New positivity bounds from the EFT hedron, talk at the 24th rencontres Itzykson of the IPHT of CEA-Saclay (2019).
  • (12) C.┬áde┬áRham, S.┬áMelville, A.J.┬áTolley and S.-Y.┬áZhou, Positivity bounds for scalar field theories, Phys. Rev. D 96 (2017) 081702 [1702.06134].
  • (13) B.┬áBellazzini, J.┬áElias┬áMir├│, R.┬áRattazzi, M.┬áRiembau and F.┬áRiva, Positive Moments for Scattering Amplitudes, 2011.00037.
  • (14) A.J.┬áTolley, Z.-Y.┬áWang and S.-Y.┬áZhou, New positivity bounds from full crossing symmetry, 2011.02400.
  • (15) I.┬áBrivio and M.┬áTrott, The Standard Model as an Effective Field Theory, Phys. Rept. 793 (2019) 1 [1706.08945].
  • (16) M.┬áCorreia, A.┬áSever and A.┬áZhiboedov, An Analytical Toolkit for the S-matrix Bootstrap, 2006.08221.
  • (17) M.┬áGell-Mann, M.┬áGoldberger and W.E.┬áThirring, Use of causality conditions in quantum theory, Phys. Rev. 95 (1954) 1612.
  • (18) J.┬áBros, H.┬áEpstein and V.┬áGlaser, A proof of the crossing property for two-particle amplitudes in general quantum field theory, Commun. Math. Phys. 1 (1965) 240.
  • (19) S.┬áCaron-Huot, Lorentzian and Analytic Bootstrap Lecture 3, 2020 Bootstrap School (2020) .
  • (20) Y.┬áJin and A.┬áMartin, Number of Subtractions in Fixed-Transfer Dispersion Relations, Phys. Rev. 135 (1964) B1375.
  • (21) A.┬áMartin, Extension of the axiomatic analyticity domain of scattering amplitudes by unitarity. 1., Nuovo Cim. A 42 (1965) 930.
  • (22) R.J.┬áEden, P.V.┬áLandshoff, D.I.┬áOlive and J.C.┬áPolkinghorne, The analytic S-matrix, Cambridge Univ. Press, Cambridge (1966).
  • (23) S.┬áCaron-Huot, D.┬áMazac, L.┬áRastelli and D.┬áSimmons-Duffin, Dispersive CFT Sum Rules, 2008.04931.
  • (24) J.┬áPenedones, J.A.┬áSilva and A.┬áZhiboedov, Nonperturbative Mellin Amplitudes: Existence, Properties, Applications, JHEP 08 (2020) 031 [1912.11100].
  • (25) H.┬áElvang, D.Z.┬áFreedman, L.-Y.┬áHung, M.┬áKiermaier, R.C.┬áMyers and S.┬áTheisen, On renormalization group flows and the a-theorem in 6d, JHEP 10 (2012) 011 [1205.3994].
  • (26) D.┬áSimmons-Duffin, A Semidefinite Program Solver for the Conformal Bootstrap, JHEP 06 (2015) 174 [1502.02033].

  • (28) L.┬áCordova, Y.┬áHe, M.┬áKruczenski and P.┬áVieira, The O(N) S-matrix Monolith, JHEP 04 (2020) 142 [1909.06495].
  • (29) M.F.┬áPaulos, J.┬áPenedones, J.┬áToledo, B.C.┬ávan Rees and P.┬áVieira, The S-matrix bootstrap. Part III: higher dimensional amplitudes, JHEP 12 (2019) 040 [1708.06765].
  • (30) A.L.┬áGuerrieri, J.┬áPenedones and P.┬áVieira, Bootstrapping QCD Using Pion Scattering Amplitudes, Phys. Rev. Lett. 122 (2019) 241604 [1810.12849].
  • (31) A.L.┬áGuerrieri, A.┬áHomrich and P.┬áVieira, Dual S-matrix Bootstrap I: 2D Theory, 2008.02770.

  • (33) J.┬áTokuda, K.┬áAoki and S.┬áHirano, Gravitational positivity bounds, 2007.15009.
  • (34) L.┬áAlberte, C.┬áde┬áRham, S.┬áJaitly and A.J.┬áTolley, Positivity Bounds and the Massless Spin-2 Pole, 2007.12667.
  • (35) B.┬áBellazzini, M.┬áLewandowski and J.┬áSerra, Positivity of Amplitudes, Weak Gravity Conjecture, and Modified Gravity, Phys. Rev. Lett. 123 (2019) 251103 [1902.03250].
